questions.explanation: The Vagus nerve arises from the side of the medulla oblongata. It innervates the larynx, trachea, oesophagus, stomach, lungs, heart and intestines. It is a mixed nerve. It controls the visceral sensations and visceral movements, i.e., heartbeat, respiratory movements, peristalsis, sound production, etc. The movement of the tongue is controlled by the hypoglossal nerve as it innervates the muscles of the tongue.

questions.text: What used to be described as Nissl’s granules in a nerve cell are now identified as _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell metabolites are the products of cellular metabolic reaction.
Fat granules are not called Nissl's granules. They are a group of specialised ribosomes.

The cell body of a nerve cell contains basophilic granules called Nissl’s granules. These bodies are particles seen within the cell bodies of neurons, consisting of granular endoplasmic reticulum and polyribosomes. They are rich in RNA and stain strongly with basic dyes.
Mitochondria are entirely different entities and are not related to Nissl's granules.

questions.explanation: The cell body of a nerve cell contains basophilic granules called Nissl’s granules. These bodies are particles seen within the cell bodies of neurons, consisting of granular endoplasmic reticulum and polyribosomes. They are rich in RNA and stain strongly with basic dyes.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct for the the node of Ranvier of the nerve?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nuerolemma is the membrane of neuron. It is not discontinuous.

At the level of the node of Ranvier, the myelin sheath is discontinuous but not the neurilemma lining. Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

options.text: Both neurilemma and myelin sheath are discontinuous.
options.explanation: Both of them are not discontinuous.

options.text: It is covered by the myelin sheath
options.explanation: It is not totally covered by myelin sheath

questions.explanation: At the level of the node of Ranvier, the myelin sheath is discontinuous but not the neurilemma lining. Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

questions.text: When we migrate from dark to light we fail to see for some time but after some time visibility becomes normal.This is an example of _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Accomodation literraly means to live or place. This does not fit the category of querry asked that's why it is incorrect.

The rod cells of the eyes contain a purplish pigment called visual purple, or rhodopsin. They function in dim light and at night. Bright light splits rhodopsin into a lipoprotein, scotopsin, and a carotenoid pigment - retinene. The splitting of rhodopsin depolarizes the rod cell. In the dark, rhodopsin is resynthesized from scotopsin and retinene. This process is called “dark adaptation.” It makes the rods functional. It takes some time for rhodopsin to be reformed. This is why on entering a dark room at daytime or on coming out of a well lighted room at night, we feel blind for a while. When we go from darkness into bright light, we feel difficulty in seeing properly for a moment till rhodopsin is bleached and cones become functional.
Mutation occurs when the sequences of amino acids fail to occur as their designated order. For example: heamophilia.
Photoperiodism is the response to changes in daylength that enables plants to adapt to seasonal changes in their environment.

questions.explanation: The rod cells of the eyes contain a purplish pigment called visual purple, or rhodopsin. They function in dim light and at night. Bright light splits rhodopsin into a lipoprotein, scotopsin, and a carotenoid pigment - retinene. The splitting of rhodopsin depolarizes the rod cell. In the dark, rhodopsin is resynthesized from scotopsin and retinene. This process is called “dark adaptation.” It makes the rods functional. It takes some time for rhodopsin to be reformed. This is why on entering a dark room at daytime or on coming out of a well lighted room at night, we feel blind for a while. When we go from darkness into bright light, we feel difficulty in seeing properly for a moment till rhodopsin is bleached and cones become functional.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is a characteristic of the human cornea?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is secreted by the conjuctiva and glandular layer.
options.explanation: The secretary glands in the eyelid and conjunctiva contain the main lacrimal gland, accessory lacrimal glands of Wolfring and Krause, goblet cells, ciliary glands of Moll and Zeis, and the meibomian gland of the tarsal plate.

options.text: It is a lacrimal gland which secretes tears.
options.explanation: The lacrimal gland plays a prominent role in the formation of the tear film, a trilaminar structure that performs the following functions: 1) provides a protective barrier for the ocular surface; 2) provides a smooth optical surface at the air-cornea interface; 3) functions as a medium for removal of debris.

options.text: Blood circulation is absent in cornea.
options.explanation: The cornea forms the anterior one-sixth of the fibrous coat. It is transparent, circular and fully visible from in front. It is composed of a particular variety of connective tissue covered externally by stratified nonkeratinized squamous epithelium and internally by simple squamous epithelium. It lacks blood vessels. It is nourished by lymph from an adjacent area.

options.text: In old age it becomes the cause of cataract.
options.explanation: Most cataracts happen because of normal changes in your eyes as you get older. When you're young, the lens in your eye is clear. Around age 40, the proteins in the lens of your eye start to break down and clump together. This clump makes a cloudy area on your lens — known as a cataract.

questions.explanation: The cornea forms the anterior one-sixth of the fibrous coat. It is transparent, circular and fully visible from in front. It is composed of a particular variety of connective tissue covered externally by stratified nonkeratinized squamous epithelium and internally by simple squamous epithelium. It lacks blood vessels. It is nourished by lymph from an adjacent area.

questions.text: Which cranial nerve has the highest number of branches?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Vagus nerve is the 10th cranial nerve and innervates the larynx, trachea, oesophagus, stomach, lungs, heart and intestines

The trigeminal nerve is the largest 5th cranial nerve. It has 3 branches – (i) Ophthalmic, a sensory branch from the skin of the nose, eyelids, forehead and scalp, and from the conjunctiva and lacrimal glands (ii) Maxillary, also a sensory branch from the skin and mucous membrane of cheeks and upper lip, and from lower eyelids (iii) Mandibular, a mixed branch innervating the lower jaw, lower lip, pinna and tongue.
The facial nerve is the 7th cranial nerve and innervates the muscles of the face and back, taste buds and salivary glands.

The trigeminal nerve is the largest 5th cranial nerve. It has 3 branches – (i) Ophthalmic, a sensory branch from the skin of the nose, eyelids, forehead and scalp, and from the conjunctiva and lacrimal glands (ii) Maxillary, also a sensory branch from the skin and mucous membrane of cheeks and upper lip, and from lower eyelids (iii) Mandibular, a mixed branch innervating the lower jaw, lower lip, pinna and tongue.

questions.explanation: The trigeminal nerve is the largest 5th cranial nerve. It has 3 branches – (i) Ophthalmic, a sensory branch from the skin of the nose, eyelids, forehead and scalp, and from the conjunctiva and lacrimal glands (ii) Maxillary, also a sensory branch from the skin and mucous membrane of cheeks and upper lip, and from lower eyelids (iii) Mandibular, a mixed branch innervating the lower jaw, lower lip, pinna and tongue. The Vagus nerve is the 10th cranial nerve and innervates the larynx, trachea, oesophagus, stomach, lungs, heart and intestines. The facial nerve is the 7th cranial nerve and innervates the muscles of the face and back, taste buds and salivary glands.

questions.text: The sympathetic nervous system induces:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Secretion of digestive juices is induced by parasympathetic nervous systems in relaxed state unlike sympathetic nervous system.

Most ganglion ibres of the sympathetic system arise from the middle protion of the spinal cord and almost terminate in ganglia that lie near the cord. This system is important during emergency situations and is associated with “ight or light.” This system accelerates the heart beat, dilates the pupil and inhibits the digestion of food etc.
Secretion of saliva is an involuntary process.
This is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is a component of the autonomic nervous system consisting of a pair of sympathetic trunks, preganglionic sympathetic fibres, postganglionic sympathetic fibres and collateral ganglia. It quickens the rate and force of heartbeat while it inhibits the secretion of saliva and gastric juice.

questions.text: The junction between the axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the next is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect.
Junction is a meeting point.
A joint is where two bones join together.

Consecutive neurons are so arranged that the axon endings of one neuron are connected to the dendrites of the next neuron. There is no cytoplasmic connection between the two neurons and microscopic gaps are left between them. Each of these contact points is known as synapse.

questions.explanation: Synapse is the close proximity of the axon of one neuron and the dendrite or cyton of another neuron with a gap of just about 200 Å in between. A nerve impulse is transmitted across the synapse by the release from the presynaptic membrane of neurotransmitter, which diffuses across the synaptic cleft to the post synaptic membrane. This triggers the propagation of the impulse from the dendrite along the length of the post synaptic neuron.

questions.text: Which of the following is regarded as a unit of nervous tissue?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neurons or nerve cells are the structural and functional units of the nervous system. These have a special structure but vary greatly in size and shape. Each neuron has a cell body which encloses cytoplasm and has a nucleus. A number of processes arise from the cell body. There is usually a single axon and a variable number of dendrites. The medullated nerve fibres are composed of a shining, white, fatty substance called myelin.
The myelin sheath is a greatly extended and modified plasma membrane wrapped around the nerve axon in a spiral fashion.
Axons are the elongated portion of the neurone located in the centre of the cell between the soma and axon terminals.

A dendrite (tree branch) is where a neuron receives input from other cells. Dendrites branch as they move towards their tips, just like tree branches do, and they even have leaf-like structures on them called spines.

questions.explanation: Neurons or nerve cells are the structural and functional units of the nervous system. These have a special structure but vary greatly in size and shape. Each neuron has a cell body which encloses cytoplasm and has a nucleus. A number of processes arise from the cell body. There is usually a single axon and a variable number of dendrites. The medullated nerve fibres are composed of a shining, white, fatty substance called myelin.
hints: as a unit of nervous tissue

questions.text: The Nissl’s granules of nerves cell are made up of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nissl granules do not contain DNA.DNA is typically found in the nucleus of the cell and is responsible for carrying the genetic information.
Nissl's granules are rich in RNA.Specifically, they contain ribosomal RNA (rRNA) which is involved in protein synthesis.
Nissl granules contain ribosomes, which are cellular structures responsible for protein synthesis. Ribosomes consist of both ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and associated proteins.
While there are some associated proteins in Nissl granules, they are not primarily composed of proteins. The main components are RNA and ribosomes.

questions.explanation: Cell body of a nerve cell contains basophilic granules called Nissl’s granules. These granules appear to be cisternae of rough endoplasmic reticulum with numerous attached and free ribosomes. They probably synthesize proteins for the cell.

questions.text: In the chemistry of vision in mammals, the photosensitive substance is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rhodopsin is a light-sensitive protein found in the rod cells of the retina in the eye. It consists of a protein called opsin bound to a molecule called retinal (a derivative of vitamin A). When retinal absorbs light, it undergoes a structural change that triggers a series of biochemical reactions leading to the generation of electrical signals, which are then transmitted to the brain for visual perception.

Melanin is a pigment found in various parts of the body, including the skin, hair, and eyes. It provides color and protection from the harmful effects of ultraviolet (UV) radiation but is not directly involved in the chemistry of vision.
Sclerotin is a protein found in the exoskeletons of some arthropods, such as insects and crustaceans. It provides structural support and protection but is not related to vision in mammals.

Retinol is a form of vitamin A, which plays a crucial role in vision. It is a precursor to the retinal molecule found in rhodopsin. Retinol itself is not directly photosensitive but is converted to retinal, which is a component of rhodopsin.

questions.explanation: Photosensitive means sensitive to light. The rod cells of retina contain a purplish pigment called rhodopsin. They function in dim light and at night. Rhodopsin consists of a protein component, opsin, linked to a nonprotein chromophore, retinal (or retinene), a derivative of vitamin A. Light falling on the rod is absorbed by the retinal, which changes its form and separates from the opsin component. This initiates the transmission of a nerve impulse to the brain.

questions.text: The vagus nerve is the cranial nerve numbering:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The seventh cranial nerve is the facial nerve, responsible for controlling the muscles of facial expression and carrying taste sensations from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue.

The vagus nerve is indeed the tenth cranial nerve. It is a long and complex nerve that plays a vital role in various functions, including regulating heart rate, controlling the muscles of the throat and voice box, and influencing the digestive system and various other internal organs.
The fifth cranial nerve is the trigeminal nerve, which is responsible for facial sensation, chewing, and some motor functions related to the jaw muscles.

The ninth cranial nerve is the glossopharyngeal nerve, involved in taste sensation at the back of the tongue, as well as controlling certain throat muscles and monitoring blood pressure and blood gases.

questions.explanation: Vagus nerve is the tenth cranial nerve. It arises from the side of medulla oblongata. It innervates the larynx, trachea, oesophagus, stomach, lungs, heart and intestines. It is a mixed nerve. It controls the visceral sensations and visceral movements, i.e., heart beat, respiratory movements, peristalsis, sound production, etc.

questions.text: How is blood supplied to the visceral organs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: By both the SNS and the PNS involuntarily
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The SNS and PNS work together to regulate blood flow to visceral organs. The sympathetic nervous system tends to increase blood flow to these organs during periods of stress or "fight or flight" responses, while the parasympathetic nervous system works to conserve energy and reduce blood flow during rest and digestion.

options.text: By the para­sympathetic nervous system involuntarily
options.explanation: The parasympathetic nervous system does play a role in regulating blood supply to visceral organs, but it works in conjunction with the sympathetic nervous system, especially during rest and digestion.

options.text: By the sympathetic nervous system involuntarily
options.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system does regulate blood flow to visceral organs, primarily during stress or emergency situations. However, it is not the sole regulator, and the parasympathetic system also plays a role.

options.text: By the sympathetic nervous system voluntarily
options.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system operates involuntarily and is not under conscious control. It responds automatically to various stimuli, such as stress or danger, and is not subject to voluntary control.

questions.explanation: The blood is supplied into visceral organs by both SNS (sympathetic nervous system) and PNS (parasympathetic nervous system) involuntarily. The sympathetic fibres increase the rate and force of the heartbeat, constrict most blood vessels and raise the arterial blood pressure. The parasympathetic fibres decrease the rate and force of the heartbeat, dilate many blood vessels and lower the arterial blood pressure.

questions.text: Ivan Pavlov performed experiments on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Simple reflexes refer to basic, involuntary responses that occur in reaction to a specific stimulus. While Pavlov's work did involve studying reflexes, his most famous experiments were focused on conditioned reflexes, which involve learning associations between stimuli and responses.

Pavlov's groundbreaking research revolved around conditioned reflexes. He discovered that dogs could be trained to associate a neutral stimulus (like the ringing of a bell) with an unconditioned stimulus (like food), leading to a conditioned response (salivating) even when the food was not present. This concept laid the foundation for the study of classical conditioning in psychology.

Cardiac reflexes pertain to the regulation of heart rate and cardiovascular responses. While Pavlov's work involved physiological responses in animals, his most famous experiments focused on conditioned reflexes rather than cardiac reflexes.
Pavlov's experiments were not related to the origin of life. His work primarily focused on the study of behavior, learning, and conditioning in animals.

questions.explanation: By training, a particular response can be obtained to a stimulus other than the one which normally evokes that response. Such a reflex is known as the conditioned reflex. The conditioned reflexes were first demonstrated in 1920’s by the Russian physiologist I.P. Pavlov. He found that the sight and smell of food reflexly cause flow of saliva in hungry animals. He rang a bell every time he offered food to a dog. The bell did not induce salivation by itself in the beginning of the experiment. Gradually, the dog learnt to associate the bell with food. Eventually, mere ringing of bell, without presenting food, induced salivation in the dog. Thus, ringing of bell can substitute sight of food to cause salivation. Pavlov called sound of the bell as conditioned stimulus, salivation in response to bell a conditioned response, food itself as unconditioned stimulus, and salivation in response to food an unconditioned response. A conditioned reflex is established when a new sensory clue (the bell) becomes associated with an inborn reflex (salivation).

questions.text: Light rays entering the eye are controlled by the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pupil is the opening in the center of the iris through which light enters the eye. It appears as a dark circular or oval shape in the middle of the colored iris. The size of the pupil is controlled by the iris muscles, which can adjust its diameter to regulate the amount of light entering the eye.

The iris is the colored part of the eye surrounding the pupil. It is composed of muscular tissue that controls the size of the pupil. The iris muscles contract or relax in response to different lighting conditions, thereby adjusting the size of the pupil and regulating the amount of light that enters the eye.

The cornea is the transparent, dome-shaped outermost layer of the eye. It covers the front portion of the eye and acts as a protective barrier. The cornea helps to focus light onto the retina at the back of the eye. However, it does not directly control the entry of light into the eye.

The lens is a clear, flexible structure located behind the iris and pupil. It plays a crucial role in focusing light onto the retina. The lens can change its shape to adjust the focal length and focus light rays onto the retina for clear vision at different distances.

questions.explanation: In the question, you are asked about the structure that controls the entry of light into the eye. The correct answer is the iris (option B), which controls the size of the pupil, thereby regulating the amount of light entering the eye.

questions.text: The retina is most sensitive at the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The optic disc is also known as the "blind spot." This region of the retina lacks photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) and is insensitive to light. It's where the optic nerve exits the eye, and no visual information is processed here.

The peripheral regions of the retina, away from the central area, have fewer cones (responsible for color vision and fine detail) and are more sensitive to detecting motion and low light conditions but are less sensitive to fine details and colors.

The macula lutea is a small, specialized area near the center of the retina that contains a high concentration of cones. While it is more sensitive than the periphery, it is not the most sensitive region for acute, detailed vision.

The fovea centralis is the central pit within the macula lutea and contains the highest concentration of cones in the retina. It is the region responsible for the sharpest and most detailed central vision, making it the most sensitive part of the retina for discerning fine details and colors.

questions.explanation: A small area of the optical part of the retina lying exactly opposite to the centre of the cornea is called the macula lutea, or yellow spot which has a yellow pigment (xanthophyll). The macula lutea has at its middle a shallow depression, the fovea centralis. The fovea has cone cells only and is the place of most distinct vision. Away from the fovea, the rod and cone cells occur in equal numbers, and at the periphery of the retina, the rods are more numerous than the cones. This is why we see better in dim light by looking out of the corner of the eye. The point on the retina from where the optic nerve starts is called the blind spot, or optic disc, as it lacks the receptor cells and is insensitive to light.

questions.text: The function of the iris is to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The movement of the lens forward and backward is primarily controlled by the ciliary muscles and suspensory ligaments in the eye, not the iris.

The main structure responsible for refracting (bending) light rays as they enter the eye is the cornea, not the iris. The iris's primary role is related to controlling the amount of light that enters the eye by adjusting the size of the pupil.

options.text: Bring about movements of eye lids
options.explanation: Eyelid movements are controlled by the muscles surrounding the eyelids, not the iris.

options.text: Alter the size of the pupil.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The iris is a colored, muscular structure within the eye that surrounds the pupil. It can contract or expand to change the size of the pupil, regulating the amount of light that enters the eye. In bright light, the iris contracts, making the pupil smaller to reduce the amount of light, while in dim light, it expands to dilate the pupil and allow more light to enter.

questions.explanation: At the junction of the sclera and the cornea, the vascular coat sharply bends into the cavity of the eyeball to form a thin, coloured partition. This partition is called iris. It is perforated at the middle by an aperture called the pupil. The iris contains two sets of smooth muscles: sphincters and dilators. These muscles regulate the amount of light entering the eyeball by varying the size of the pupil. The sphincter muscles are arranged in rings. Their contraction makes the pupil smaller in bright light so that less light enters the eye. The dilator muscles are arranged radially. Their contraction widens the pupil in dim light to let in more light. Iris, by regulating the size of the pupil, allows light to pass only through the centre of the lens, which is optically the most effective part.

questions.text: The iris is part of the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sclera, commonly known as the "white of the eye," is the tough, outermost layer of the eye. It provides structural support and protection to the eye but does not include the iris.

The choroid is a vascular layer located between the sclera and the retina. It supplies blood to the retina and contains pigments that help absorb excess light, enhancing vision quality. The iris is a part of the choroid because it is situated in the front of the choroid and helps regulate the amount of light entering the eye.

This is the correct answer. The choroid, as mentioned, is the vascular layer that includes the iris, while the retina is the innermost layer of the eye that contains the photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) responsible for capturing light and initiating visual signals.
This option is not accurate because the iris is not a part of the sclera (sclerotic) but is indeed a part of the choroid.

questions.explanation: At the junction of the sclera and the cornea, the vascular coat sharply bends into the cavity of the eyeball to form a thin, coloured partition. This partition is called iris. It is perforated at the middle by an aperture called the pupil. The iris contains two sets of smooth muscles: sphincters and dilators. These muscles regulate the amount of light entering the eyeball by varying the size of the pupil. The sphincter muscles are arranged in rings. Their contraction makes the pupil smaller in bright light so that less light enters the eye. The dilator muscles are arranged radially. Their contraction widens the pupil in dim light to let in more light. Iris, by regulating the size of the pupil, allows light to pass only through the centre of the lens, which is optically the most effective part.

questions.text: Afferent nerve fibrers carry impulses from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not correct. Efferent nerve fibers transmit impulses from the central nervous system (CNS) to effector organs (muscles or glands) to initiate a response.

This is the correct answer. Afferent nerve fibers, also known as sensory nerve fibers, carry sensory information (impulses) from receptors in various parts of the body (such as the skin, eyes, ears, etc.) to the central nervous system (CNS) for processing and interpretation.
This is not accurate. Nerve fibers that transmit impulses from the CNS to receptors are typically efferent (motor) fibers, which initiate responses or actions in the periphery.
Efferent nerve fibers carry impulses from the CNS to muscles to control movements or actions. These fibers are responsible for motor functions, not sensory input.

questions.explanation: Afferent nerve fibers carry impulses from the receptors to the central nervous system. Efferent nerve fibers conduct nerve impulses from the central nervous system to the effector organs such as muscles and glands.

questions.text: The hypoglossal nerve is the _ cranial nerve.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The 10th cranial nerve is the vagus nerve, responsible for various functions in the body, including regulating the heart, lungs, and digestive system.
The 9th cranial nerve is the glossopharyngeal nerve, involved in taste sensation, controlling certain throat muscles, and monitoring blood pressure and blood gases.
The 7th cranial nerve is the facial nerve, responsible for controlling the muscles of facial expression and carrying taste sensations from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue.
This is the correct answer. The 12th cranial nerve is the hypoglossal nerve, which controls the muscles of the tongue and plays a crucial role in speech and swallowing.

questions.explanation: The hypoglossal nerve is the twelfth paired cranial nerve. Its name is derived from ancient Greek, 'hypo' meaning under, and 'glossal' meaning tongue. The nerve has a purely somatic motor function, innervating all the extrinsic and intrinsic muscles of the tongue (except the palatoglossus, innervated by vagus nerve).

questions.text: The third ventricle of the brain is also known as the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term is not a recognized anatomical structure or name for any part of the brain's ventricular system.
Similarly,"Rhinocoel" is not a recognized term for any brain ventricle.
"Paracoel" is also not a recognized anatomical term for any part of the brain's ventricular system.

This is the correct answer. The third ventricle of the brain is often referred to as the "Diacoel." It is one of the four interconnected cavities within the brain that contain cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and play a role in the circulation and protection of the brain and spinal cord.

questions.explanation: The ventricles consist of four hollow, fluid-filled spaces inside the brain. The third ventricle is also known as diacoel. The third ventricle consists of a narrow channel between the hemispheres through the area of the thalamus. It is connected by the cerebral aqueduct or aqueduct of Sylvius or iter in the midbrain portion of the brainstem to the fourth ventricle in the pons and medulla. Metacoel is the IV ventricle, rhinocoel is the I ventricle and paracoel is the II ventricle.

questions.text: One function of the parasympathetic nervous system is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The contraction of hair muscles, also known as arrector pili muscles, is primarily regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. It causes hair to stand on end, a response known as piloerection.

Sweat gland activity is mainly regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. The sympathetic system promotes the production and release of sweat in response to heat or stress, not the parasympathetic system.
The acceleration of the heart rate is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system. It triggers the "fight or flight" response, increasing heart rate to prepare the body for action.

This is the correct answer. The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for constricting the pupil (making it smaller) in response to non-stressful, restful situations. This action allows for better near vision and reduces the amount of light entering the eye.

questions.explanation: The action of the parasympathetic nervous system is opposite to that of the sympathetic nervous system. If the sympathetic nervous system accelerates an action, the parasympathetic nervous system slows it. However, neither system is exclusively exitatory or inhibitory. The parasympathetic fibres constrict the pupil, decrease the rate and force of heart beat, dilate many blood vessels, lower the arterial blood pressure, quicken the peristaltic movements, and contract the urinary bladder.

questions.text: Which of the following cranial nerves can regulate heart beat?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The vagus nerve is the cranial nerve responsible for regulating heart rate. It has parasympathetic fibers that can slow down the heart rate, promoting relaxation and reducing heart rate during periods of rest and recovery.
The glossopharyngeal nerve is involved in various functions, including taste sensation and monitoring blood pressure. However, it does not have a direct role in regulating heart rate.
The vestibulocochlear nerve is responsible for hearing and balance and is not involved in regulating heart rate.
The facial nerve controls the muscles of facial expression and carries taste sensations from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. It does not have a direct role in regulating heart rate.

questions.explanation: The vagus nerve is one of the nerves responsible for controlling your heart rate. This important nerve passes through your neck and thorax to your abdomen and has an extensive distribution throughout your body. Stimulation of the vagus nerve with the Valsalva manoeuvre can help control heart palpitations.

questions.text: The sensitive pigmented layer of the eye is the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cornea is the clear, transparent outermost layer of the eye responsible for refracting (bending) light entering the eye. It is not a pigmented layer but rather a transparent structure.

The retina is the innermost layer of the eye that contains light-sensitive photoreceptor cells (rods and cones). These cells are responsible for capturing light and converting it into electrical signals that are sent to the brain for visual perception. The retina contains pigmented cells as well, which help absorb excess light.

The sclera, also known as the "white of the eye," is the tough, fibrous outer layer of the eye that provides structural support and protection. It is not a pigmented layer and is typically white in color.

The iris is the colored, muscular structure in the eye that surrounds the pupil. While it contains pigmented cells that determine the color of the eye, its primary function is to regulate the size of the pupil to control the amount of light entering the eye. It is not the sensitive pigmented layer responsible for vision.

questions.explanation: The retina consists of both the pigmented layer and the sensory layer. The pigment cells reinforce the light-absorbing property of the choroid in reducing the scattering of light in the eye. The sensory layer consists of rods and cones required for vision.

questions.text: GnRH a hypothalamic hormone, needed in reproduction acts on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Anterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and FSH
options.explanation: GnRH (Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone) is indeed a hypothalamic hormone involved in reproduction.It primarily acts on the anterior pituitary gland.GnRH stimulates the secretion of LH (Luteinizing Hormone) and FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) from the anterior pituitary gland.

options.text: Posterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of oxytocin and FSH
options.explanation: GnRH does not directly stimulate the posterior pituitary gland.FSH secretion is primarily regulated by GnRH and occurs in the anterior pituitary gland, not the posterior pituitary gland.Oxytocin is released from the posterior pituitary gland, but GnRH does not stimulate its secretion.

options.text: Posterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and relaxin
options.explanation: GnRH typically does not stimulate the posterior pituitary gland.Relaxin is not a hormone typically stimulated by GnRH.

options.text: Anterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and oxytocin.
options.explanation: GnRH acts on the anterior pituitary gland.It stimulates the secretion of LH and FSH, not oxytocin.

questions.explanation: Gonadotropin releasing hormone (GnRH) is secreted by the hypothalamus which stimulates the anterior lobe of pituitary gland to secrete luteinising hormone (LH) and Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH).

questions.text: Hypersecretion of growth hormone in adults does not cause further increase in height because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Epiphyseal plates are areas of cartilage at the ends of long bones where growth occurs. They typically close or ossify after adolescence, which means they turn into bone and no longer allow for further increase in height.

options.text: Bones loose their sensitivity to growth hormone in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not entirely accurate. While it's true that the growth plates close after adolescence, it's not because bones lose their sensitivity to growth hormone. Instead, it's due to the natural developmental process.

options.text: Muscle fibres do not grow in size after birth
options.explanation: This statement is generally true. Muscle fibers can grow in size through hypertrophy, but this process is not primarily regulated by growth hormone. It's not directly related to height increase or the lack thereof.

options.text: Growth hormone becomes inactive in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Growth hormone remains active in adults, although its role shifts from primarily promoting longitudinal bone growth (height) during childhood and adolescence to other functions in adulthood, such as maintaining muscle mass and bone density.

questions.explanation: Epiphyseal plate is a hyaline cartilage plate in the metaphysis at each end of long bone. It is part of long bone where new bone growth takes place. In adults, elevated levels of GH results in acromegaly where no increase in height occurs because of ossified epiphyseal plate.

questions.text: A temporary endocrine gland in the human body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The corpus cardiacum is a structure found in insects, not in humans. It's part of the insect endocrine system and is responsible for storing and releasing neurohormones.

The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine gland in the human body. It forms in the ovary after ovulation when a mature follicle transforms into a structure that produces hormones, including progesterone, to support early pregnancy. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum breaks down.
The corpus allatum is also a structure found in insects and not in humans. It's part of the insect endocrine system and is involved in producing juvenile hormones that influence insect development.

The pineal gland is a small endocrine gland in the human brain. While it's not typically described as "temporary," it plays a crucial role in regulating the sleep-wake cycle and producing the hormone melatonin, which is involved in regulating sleep and circadian rhythms.

questions.explanation: Corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine gland in human females. It secretes progesterone which stimulates the uterine glands to produce increased amount of watery mucus and is also essential for maintenance of endothelium. In absence of fertilisation, corpus luteum disintegrates leading to menstruation.

questions.text: Graves’ disease is caused due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hyposecretion means the thyroid gland is not producing enough thyroid hormones.Graves' disease is actually characterized by hypersecretion (overproduction) of thyroid hormones, not hyposecretion.

This is the correct answer. Graves' disease is caused by an autoimmune response where the immune system stimulates the thyroid gland to produce excessive amounts of thyroid hormones, leading to hyperthyroidism.
Adrenal gland hyposecretion is unrelated to Graves' disease. The adrenal glands primarily produce hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, not thyroid hormones.

Adrenal gland hypersecretion is also unrelated to Graves' disease. An overactive adrenal gland might result in excessive production of adrenal hormones, but this condition is distinct from Graves' disease.

questions.explanation: Exophthalmic goitre or Graves’ disease is a thyroid enlargement (goitre) in which the thyroid secretes excessive amount of thyroid hormone. It is characterised by exophthalmia (protrusion of eye balls because of fluid accumulation behind them), loss of weight, slight rise in the body temperature, excitability, rapid heart beat, nervousness and restlessness.

questions.text: Name a peptide hormone which acts mainly on hepatocytes,adipocytes and enhances cellular glucose uptake and utilisation:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin is produced by the pancreas and plays a crucial role in regulating blood sugar levels. It promotes glucose uptake by cells, including hepatocytes (liver cells) and adipocytes (fat cells), facilitating glucose utilization and storage.

Glucagon is another hormone produced by the pancreas, but it has the opposite effect of insulin. It raises blood sugar levels by promoting the release of glucose from the liver. It does not enhance cellular glucose uptake.

Secretin is a hormone produced by the small intestine that primarily stimulates the pancreas to release bicarbonate, aiding in neutralizing stomach acid. It does not have a direct role in glucose uptake or utilization.
Gastrin is produced by the stomach and plays a role in regulating stomach acid secretion. It is not involved in glucose metabolism or cellular glucose uptake.

questions.explanation: Insulin is a peptide hormone, secreted by the b cells of the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas, that promotes the uptake of glucose by body cells, particularly in the liver (hepatocytes) and muscles (adipocytes), and thereby controls its concentration in the blood.

questions.text: The posterior pituitary gland is not a ‘true’ endocrine gland because :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is provided with a duct
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The posterior pituitary gland is not a true endocrine gland because it does not have its own duct. Instead, it releases hormones directly into the bloodstream.

options.text: It only stores and releases hormones
options.explanation: This statement is also correct. The posterior pituitary gland primarily stores and releases hormones produced by the hypothalamus, such as oxytocin and vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone). It does not produce hormones on its own.

options.text: It is under the regulation of hypothalamus
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. The posterior pituitary gland is controlled by the hypothalamus, which produces and sends hormones to the posterior pituitary for storage and release. The hypothalamus acts as a control center for the release of these hormones.
This statement is incorrect. The posterior pituitary gland does not secrete enzymes; its main function is to store and release hormones.

questions.explanation: Posterior lobe of pituitary gland does not secrete any hormone. Its hormones are synthesised by the hypothalamus. It only stores and releases these hormones. Hence, it cannot be considered as true gland.

questions.text: Which of the following pairs of hormones are not antagonistic (having opposite effects) to each other?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aldosterone is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands that promotes the reabsorption of sodium ions in the kidneys, leading to an increase in blood volume and blood pressure. Atrial Natriuretic Factor (ANF), on the other hand, is produced by the atria of the heart and has the opposite effect. ANF promotes the excretion of sodium and water by the kidneys, which decreases blood volume and blood pressure. Therefore, Aldosterone and ANF are antagonistic hormones.

Relaxin is a hormone that plays a role in the relaxation of uterine muscles during pregnancy. Inhibin is a hormone that inhibits the production of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) in the anterior pituitary gland. These hormones have different functions and do not have opposite effects on the same physiological process. Therefore, Relaxin and Inhibin are not antagonistic hormones.

Parathormone (also known as parathyroid hormone) is produced by the parathyroid glands and is involved in increasing blood calcium levels. Calcitonin, on the other hand, is produced by the thyroid gland and has the opposite effect by decreasing blood calcium levels. Therefore, Parathormone and Calcitonin are antagonistic hormones.

Insulin is produced by the pancreas and lowers blood glucose levels by facilitating the uptake of glucose into cells. Glucagon, also produced by the pancreas, raises blood glucose levels by promoting the release of stored glucose from the liver. Insulin and glucagon are antagonistic hormones.

questions.explanation: Relaxin hormone is secreted by ovary and placenta during pregnancy, which relaxes ligaments in pelvis and softens and widens cervix during childbirth. Inhibin secreted by granulosa cells in the ovaries inhibits secretion of FSH by anterior pituitary. Thus, relaxin and inhibin have different functions and are not antagonistic.

questions.text: The amino acid tryptophan is the precursor for the synthesis of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tryptophan is not a direct precursor for estrogen and progesterone. These female sex hormones are primarily synthesized from cholesterol.
Tryptophan is not the precursor for cortisol and cortisone either. These are corticosteroid hormones that are synthesized from cholesterol and involve multiple enzymatic steps.

This is the correct option. Tryptophan is a precursor for both melatonin and serotonin. Serotonin is a neurotransmitter, and it can be converted into melatonin, a hormone that regulates sleep-wake cycles.
Tryptophan is not the precursor for thyroid hormones like thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3). These hormones are synthesized from iodine and the amino acid tyrosine.

questions.explanation: Tryptophan is an essential amino acid which is the precursor for the synthesis of melatonin and serotonin.

questions.text: Which one of the following hormones is not involved in sugar metabolism?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas, and it plays a crucial role in sugar metabolism. Its main function is to lower blood sugar levels by facilitating the uptake of glucose into cells, where it can be used for energy or stored as glycogen.

Glucagon is another hormone produced by the pancreas, but it has the opposite effect of insulin. Glucagon raises blood sugar levels by promoting the breakdown of glycogen into glucose in the liver and stimulating the release of glucose into the bloodstream.

Cortisone is a corticosteroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands. While cortisone does have various metabolic effects, it primarily regulates inflammation and has a relatively minor role in sugar metabolism compared to insulin and glucagon.

Aldosterone is also a hormone produced by the adrenal glands, but it primarily regulates salt and water balance in the body, particularly in the kidneys. It does not have a direct role in sugar metabolism.

questions.explanation: Aldosterone (saltretaining hormone) is the principal mineralocorticoid in humans, secreted by adrenal cortex. Its main function is to regulate sodium content of the body.

questions.text: Which one of the following hormones is released by the posterior part of pituitary gland?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prolactin is not released by the posterior pituitary but by the anterior pituitary gland. It primarily stimulates milk production in the mammary glands.
MSH is produced by cells in the anterior pituitary and not the posterior pituitary. It plays a role in regulating skin pigmentation.

This is the correct answer. ADH is produced by the neurons in the posterior pituitary. It regulates water balance in the body by influencing the reabsorption of water in the kidneys, thereby reducing urine production and helping to maintain blood pressure.

LH is also released by the anterior pituitary, not the posterior pituitary. In males, LH stimulates the production of testosterone, and in females, it triggers ovulation and supports the formation of the corpus luteum.

questions.explanation: Two hormones, i.e oxytocin (OT) and antidiuretic hormone (ADH) are synthesised in the hypothalamus, but stored and released by the posterior lobe of pituitary gland. Hypothalamus is regarded as the Master Gland.

questions.text: A chemical signal that has both endocrine and neural roles is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Epinephrine is produced by the adrenal glands and acts as both a hormone and a neurotransmitter. In its endocrine role, it is released into the bloodstream during the "fight or flight" response, increasing heart rate, dilating airways, and mobilizing energy stores in the body. As a neurotransmitter, it is released by nerve cells in the sympathetic nervous system to transmit signals between nerve cells.

Cortisol is a steroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands. Its primary role is as an endocrine hormone, where it regulates various metabolic processes, including glucose metabolism and immune response. While it can indirectly affect the nervous system, it doesn't have a direct neural role like epinephrine.

Melatonin is primarily a hormone produced by the pineal gland in the brain's endocrine system. It plays a key role in regulating the sleep-wake cycle (circadian rhythm) but does not have a significant neural role like epinephrine.

Calcitonin is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland. Its primary function is to regulate calcium levels in the blood by promoting calcium deposition in bone tissue. It does not have a neural role like epinephrine.

questions.explanation: The hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine are secreted from the adrenal medulla. They are emergency hormones released in condition of stress, emergency etc. Epinephrine and norepinephrine are also released by the adrenergic nerve fibres of the sympathetic nervous system where they act as neurotransmitters.

questions.text: Identify the hormone with its correct matching of source and function:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Oxytocin - posterior pituitary - growth and maintenance of mammary glands
options.explanation: Oxytocin is indeed produced in the posterior pituitary. However, its primary function is not the growth and maintenance of mammary glands. Oxytocin's main functions include uterine contractions during childbirth and the ejection of milk during breastfeeding.

options.text: Melatonin - ­pineal gland - regulates the normal rhythm of sleepwake cycle.
options.explanation: This is correct. Melatonin is produced by the pineal gland and is primarily responsible for regulating the normal rhythm of the sleep-wake cycle (circadian rhythm).

options.text: Progesterone - corpus luteum-stimulation of growth and activities of female secondary sex organs
options.explanation: The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine structure formed in the ovary after ovulation. Progesterone is produced by the corpus luteum and plays a key role in the preparation of the uterus for possible implantation of a fertilized egg. It also helps maintain pregnancy and supports the growth and activities of female secondary sex organs.

options.text: Atrial natriuretic factor - ventricular wall - increases the blood pressure.
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Atrial Natriuretic Factor (ANF), also known as atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP), is primarily produced and released by the atria of the heart, not the ventricular wall. Its main function is to reduce blood pressure and increase sodium excretion by the kidneys, which helps regulate fluid balance and lower blood pressure.

questions.explanation: Oxytocin is produced by the hypothalamus and generally secreted by the posterior pituitary. It stimulates the secretion of milk from mammary glands; causes contraction of the uterus at the time of childbirth. Progesterone is secreted by the corpus luteum. It stimulates the uterus for pregnancy, implantation, formation of the placenta and development of mammary glands. The atrial natriuretic factor is secreted by the atrial wall in response to an increased return of the venous blood. This hormone regulates the blood volume through increased excretion of ions and water.

questions.text: The figure shows the human urinary system with structures labelled A to D. Select the correct option.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: C­ - Medulla­ - inner zone of the kidney containing complete nephrons
D ­- Cortex ­- outer part of kidney that does not contain any part of nephrons
A ­- Adrenal gland ­- located at the anterior part of the kidney. Secretes catecholamines which stimulate glycogen breakdown.
options.explanation: In the given figure, A is an adrenal gland which secretes two catecholamines: adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline (norepinephrine). Adrenaline increases the conversion of glycogen to glucose providing quick energy for a “fight or flight” response. B is the renal pelvis which is a sac-like cavity of the kidney leading to ureters that is not directly connected to the loop of Henle. C is the medulla, the inner region of the kidney containing loop of Henle, collecting ducts and ducts of Bellini. D is the cortex that has proximal and distal convoluted tubules and contains Malpighian corpuscles.

options.text: B ­- Pelvis -­ broad funnel-shaped space inner to the hilum, directly connected to the loops of Henle.

questions.explanation: In the given figure, A is an adrenal gland which secretes two catecholamines: adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline (norepinephrine). Adrenaline increases the conversion of glycogen to glucose providing quick energy for a “fight or flight” response. B is the renal pelvis which is a sac-like cavity of the kidney leading to ureters that is not directly connected to the loop of Henle. C is the medulla, the inner region of the kidney containing loop of Henle, collecting ducts and ducts of Bellini. D is the cortex that has proximal and distal convoluted tubules and contains Malpighian corpuscles.

questions.text: A pregnant female delivers a baby who suffers from stunted growth, mental retardation, a low intelligence quotient and abnormal skin.This is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thyroid cancer typically leads to an overproduction of thyroid hormones rather than a deficiency. Therefore, it is not likely to be the cause of the described symptoms.

The pars distalis is a part of the anterior pituitary gland that secretes various hormones, but it does not produce thyroid hormones directly. Oversecretion of hormones from the pars distalis could lead to other issues but is not directly related to the symptoms described.

This is the correct answer. Iodine is an essential nutrient required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones (T3 and T4). A deficiency of iodine in the diet can lead to hypothyroidism, particularly in pregnant women, which can result in the described symptoms in the newborn. This condition is known as congenital hypothyroidism or cretinism.

Growth hormone deficiency can result in stunted growth, but it is not typically associated with the specific combination of symptoms mentioned, such as mental retardation, low intelligence quotient, and abnormal skin.

questions.explanation: Iodine is needed for the synthesis of T3 and T4. Iodine binds to the tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin, which is then hydrolysed into iodotyrosines that combine to form tri-iodothyronine (T3 ) or thyroxine (tetraiodothyronine or T4 ). Therefore, deficiency of iodine in the diet of a pregnant female will lead to the improper synthesis of thyroid hormones in the newly borne infant.
hints: stunted growth, mental retardation, a low intelligence quotient

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct in relation to the endocrine system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Non­-nutrient chemicals produced by the body in trace amounts that act as intercellular messengers are known as hormones.
Inhibitory hormones are produced by the pituitary gland.
Adenohypophysis is under direct neural regulation of the hypothalamus.
Organs in the body like the gastrointestinal tract,heart,kidney and liver do not produce any hormones.

questions.explanation: Releasing and inhibiting factors are released by the hypothalamus. The hypothalamus is connected to adenohypophysis by hypophysial portal vein and is connected to the neurohypophysis by axons of neurosecretory cells. Hence, neurohypophysis is directly under neural control. The cardiocytes of the atria of the heart secrete peptide hormone, called atrial natriuretic factor (ANF) in response to an increased return of the deoxygenated (venous) blood. The liver produces angiotensinogen which is changed to angiotensin II by an enzyme renin secreted by juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA).

questions.text: Select the answer which correctly matches the endocrine gland with the hormone it secretes and its function/deficiency symptom.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Iodine is required for production of thyroxine, thus lack of iodine results in hyposecretion of thyroxine. To compensate, thyroid gland enlarges and the condition is known as goitre. Corpus luteum secretes progesterone which maintains uterine endothelium and mucus secretion in uterus, Fallopian tubes and vagina. Oxytocin stimulates uterine contractions but is secreted by posterior pituitary. Anterior pituitary secretes GH, whose oversecretion causes abnormal growth.

questions.explanation: Iodine is required for production of thyroxine, thus lack of iodine results in hyposecretion of thyroxine. To compensate, thyroid gland enlarges and the condition is known as goitre. Corpus luteum secretes progesterone which maintains uterine endothelium and mucus secretion in uterus, Fallopian tubes and vagina. Oxytocin stimulates uterine contractions but is secreted by posterior pituitary. Anterior pituitary secretes GH, whose oversecretion causes abnormal growth
hints: hormone it secretes and its function/deficiency

questions.text: Select the option which correctly matches the endocrine gland with its hormone and its function :
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Interstitial cells (or Leydig ‘s cells) are the cells interspersed between the seminiferous tubules of the testis. They secrete androgens including testosterone in response to stimulation by luteinizing hormone from the anterior pitutary gland. Androgens produce and maintain male characteristics and stimulate germinal epithelium to undergo spermatogenesis.

questions.text: Norepinephrine (i)is released by sympathetic fibers (ii)is released by parasympathetic fibers (iii)increases the heart rate (iv)decreases blood pressureWhich of the above statements are correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sympathetic fibers are also called the small fiber nerves. It is developed by the collections of nerve clusters called ganglia. It is available on the outside of the spinal cord. It will increase the rate of heart beat, muscle contraction and rate of conduction. So, i is correct. And iii is also correct.

The nerve fibers of the parasympathetic fibers nervous system are cranial nerves, vagus nerve and lumber of spinal nerve. During this nerve stimulation it increases the digestive secretion and also reduces the heartbeat. Norepinephrine hormone will increase the heart beat. So, ii is not correct and iii is correct.
Sympathetic fibers release the norepinephrine. Blood pressure is increased during the large amount of norepinephrine hormone excretion. So, i is correct but iv is not the correct answer.
Parasympathetic fibers do not produce the norepinephrine hormone. Decreasing blood pressure is not possible during the excretion of hormones. So, both ii and iv are not correct.

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is secreted by some neurons of the sympathetic nervous system and also by adrenal medulla. It accelerates heart rate.

questions.text: Which of the following represents the action of insulin?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Increases blood glucose level by stimulating glucagon production.
Decreases blood glucose levels by forming glycogen.
Increases blood glucose levels by promoting cellular uptake of glucose
Increases blood glucose levels by hydrolysis of glycogen

questions.explanation: Insulin is a peptide hormone, secreted by the b cells of the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas, that promotes the uptake of glucose by body cells, particularly in the liver (hepatocytes) and muscles (adipocytes), and thereby controls its concentration in the blood.

questions.text: A person entering an empty room suddenly finds a snake right in front of him.Which one of the following is likely to happen in his neuro­hormonal control system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla
Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly across the cleft and transmit a nerve impulse.
Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division of brain
Sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal cortex.

questions.explanation: Hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine are secreted from adrenal medulla. They are emergency hormones released in condition of stress, emergency etc. Epinephrine and norepinephrine are also released by adrenergic nerve fibres of sympathetic nervous system where they act as neurotransmitters.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs of hormones are the examples of those that can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it(mostly in the nucleus)?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin has the receptor on the outer surface of cells which let them cross the barrier easily
Insulin has the receptor on the outer surface of cells which let them cross the barrier easily
Oxytocin has its receptors on myometrium of uterus.

Steroid hormones such as cortisol, testosterone, estradiol and progesterone, mostly regulate gene expression or chromosome function by the interaction of the hormone-receptor complex with the genome. So, these can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it.

questions.explanation: Steroid hormones such as cortisol, testosterone, estradiol and progesterone, mostly regulate gene expression or chromosome function by the interaction of the hormone-receptor complex with the genome. So, these can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it.

questions.text: Which statement is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Glucagon is secreted by the b-­cells of islets of Langerhans and stimulates glycogenolysis.
Secretion of thymosins is stimulated with aging
In females, FSH first binds with specific receptors on ovarian cell membrane
options.explanation: Hormone action involves their reception by target cells. Specific proteins called hormone receptors that are located in target tissues only bind with these hormones. Hormone receptor may be of two types: membrane-bound receptor and intracellular receptors. Steroid hormones etc., bind with intracellular receptors while some hormones e.g., pituitary hormones like FSH etc., bind with membrane-bound receptors.

options.text: FSH stimulates the secretion of estrogen and progesterone.
options.explanation: FSH stimulates the production of estrogen which in turn inhibits FSH. Estrogen stimulates Progesterone which in turn inhibits estrogen.

questions.explanation: Hormone action involves their reception by target cells. Specific proteins called hormone receptors that are located in target tissues only bind with these hormones. Hormone receptor may be of two types: membrane-bound receptor and intracellular receptors. Steroid hormones etc., bind with intracellular receptors while some hormones e.g., pituitary hormones like FSH etc., bind with membrane-bound receptors.

questions.text: Which option is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The posterior lobe of the pituitary stores and releases two hormones called oxytocin and vasopressin. These hormones are actually produced by the neurosecretory cells in the hypothalamus and stand in the terminals of their axons that pass into the posterior lobe through a stalk. They are released via the posterior lobe when required. Vasopressin is also called the antidiuretic hormone (ADH). It decreases the loss of water in the urine by increasing reabsorption of water in the distal convoluted tubules, collecting tubules and collecting ducts in the kidneys.

questions.explanation: The posterior lobe of the pituitary stores and releases two hormones called oxytocin and vasopressin. These hormones are actually produced by the neurosecretory cells in the hypothalamus and stand in the terminals of their axons that pass into the posterior lobe through a stalk. They are released via the posterior lobe when required. Vasopressin is also called the antidiuretic hormone (ADH). It decreases the loss of water in the urine by increasing reabsorption of water in the distal convoluted tubules, collecting tubules and collecting ducts in the kidneys.

questions.text: The 24 hour (diurnal) rhythm of our body such as the sleep­wake cycle is regulated by which hormone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Calcitonin is a hormone that your thyroid gland makes and releases to help regulate calcium levels in your blood by decreasing it.
Prolactin is a polypeptide hormone that is responsible for lactation, breast development, and hundreds of other actions needed to maintain homeostasis.

Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone and medication which is involved in regulating visceral functions. It appears as a white microcrystalline granule. Adrenaline is normally produced by the adrenal glands and by a small number of neurons in the medulla oblongata.

Melatonin is a hormone secreted by the pineal gland and retinas of vertebrates. Melatonin secretion by the pineal gland is linked to the darklight cycle of the organism’s environment, being greatest at night and lowest by day. The hormone is involved in regulating certain diurnal and seasonal changes in the body, such as the reproductive cycle in seasonally breeding animals. It is used as a drug to treat sleep disorders and symptoms of jet lag.

questions.explanation: Melatonin is a hormone secreted by the pineal gland and retinas of vertebrates. Melatonin secretion by the pineal gland is linked to the darklight cycle of the organism’s environment, being greatest at night and lowest by day. The hormone is involved in regulating certain diurnal and seasonal changes in the body, such as the reproductive cycle in seasonally breeding animals. It is used as a drug to treat sleep disorders and symptoms of jet lag.

questions.text: Given below is an incomplete table on hormones, their source glands and one major effect of each human body. Identify the correct option.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The correct answers for the three blanks A, B and C are ovary, glucagon and growth hormone respectively.

questions.explanation: The correct answers for the three blanks A, B and C are ovary, glucagon and growth hormone respectively.

questions.text: Injury to adrenal cortex is not likely to affect the secretion of which one of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aldosterone is the main mineralocorticoid steroid hormone produced by the zona glomerulosa of the adrenal cortex in the adrenal gland. It is essential for sodium conservation in the kidney, salivary glands, sweat glands, and colon.
Androstenedione and dehydroepiandrosterone, which are weak androgens arising from the adrenal cortex, constitute major sources of androgens in women.

Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone and medication which is involved in regulating visceral functions. It appears as a white microcrystalline granule. Adrenaline is normally produced by the adrenal glands and by a small number of neurons in the medulla oblongata.
Cortisol is a steroid hormone that is produced by your 2 adrenal glands, which sit on top of each kidney. When you are stressed, increased cortisol is released into your bloodstream.

questions.explanation: Adrenal glands or (suprarenal glands) are two triangular endocrine glands, each of which covers the superior surface of a kidney. Each gland has two parts, the medulla and cortex.The medulla forms the grey core of the gland; and produces adrenaline and noradrenaline.The cortex is a yellowish tissue surrounding the medulla. It produces three kinds of corticosteroid hormones, which affect carbohydrate metabolism (e.g., cortisol), electrolyte metabolism (e.g., aldosterone), and the sex glands (oestrogens and androgens). Thus injury to adrenal cortex is not likely to affect the secretion of adrenaline.

questions.text: Low Ca+ in the body fluid may be the cause of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Low levels of blood Ca++ ions stimulate the parathyroid directly to increase parathormone production whereas high evels of Ca++ ions suppress its release. Under-activity causes a drop in blood Ca++ ions which in turn leads to muscular tetany.
Angina pectoris—or simply angina—is chest pain or discomfort that keeps coming back.

Gout is a common and complex form of arthritis that can affect anyone. It's characterized by sudden, severe attacks of pain, swelling, redness and tenderness in one or more joints, most often in the big toe.

questions.explanation: Tetany is a spasm and twitching of the muscles, particularly those of the face, hands, and feet. Tetany is usually caused by a reduction in the blood calcium level, which may be due to underactive parathyroid glands, rickets, or alkalosis.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs is incorrectly matched?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glucagon is secreted by alpha cells of Islets of Langerhans.

Somatostatin is a cyclic peptide well known for its strong regulatory effects throughout the body. Also known by the name of growth hormone inhibiting hormone, it is produced in many locations, which include the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, pancreas, hypothalamus, and central nervous system (CNS).

Relaxin is produced primarily by the corpus luteum, in both pregnant and nonpregnant females. It attains the highest plasma levels during pregnancy. In this condition, relaxin is also produced by the decidua and placenta.

Diabetes mellitus (diabetes) is a chronic and potentially life-threatening condition where the body loses its ability to produce insulin, or begins to produce or use insulin less efficiently, resulting in blood glucose levels that are too high (hyperglycaemia).

questions.explanation: Glucagon is secreted by alpha-cells in the Isles of Langerhans, while beta-cells produce insulin.

questions.text: Toxic agents present in food which interfere with thyroxine synthesis lead to the development of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Toxic nodular goiter involves an enlarged thyroid gland that contains a small rounded mass or masses called nodules, which produce too much thyroid hormone.

Cretinism is a disease caused by hypothyroidism. Iodine deficiency in diet during pregnancy is the major cause of cretinism. Hypothyroidism leads to impaired development of the growing fetus and leads to mental retardation and stunted growth. It is a congenital disorder.

Iodine is required for production of thyroxine, thus lack of iodine results in hyposecretion of thyroxine. To compensate, thyroid gland enlarges and the condition is known as goitre. Corpus luteum secretes progesterone which maintains uterine endothelium and mucus secretion in uterus, Fallopian tubes and vagina. Oxytocin stimulates uterine contractions but is secreted by posterior pituitary. Anterior pituitary secretes GH, whose oversecretion causes abnormal growth.

questions.text: Select the correct matching of a hormone its source and function.

options.explanation: Vasopressin is a pituitary hormone that is also known as the antidiuretic hormone (ADH). It is synthesized in the hypothalamus whereas it is secreted from the posterior pituitary gland. Its primary function is to promote the water reabsorption in the renal tubule of kidneys.

Norepinephrine is continuously released into circulation at low levels while epinephrine is only released during times of stress. Norepinephrine is also known as noradrenaline. It is both a hormone and the most common neurotransmitter of the sympathetic nervous system.

Glucagon is a peptide hormone, produced by alpha cells of the pancreas. It raises the concentration of glucose and fatty acids in the bloodstream and is considered to be the main catabolic hormone of the body. It is also used as a medication to treat a number of health conditions.
Prolactin is a polypeptide hormone that is responsible for lactation, breast development, and hundreds of other actions needed to maintain homeostasis.

questions.explanation: Vasopressin reduces water loss through urine by stimulating resorption of water by the distal tubules of the kidney. Glucagon is released from a alpha-cells. Prolactin is produced from anterior pituitary.

questions.subject: Biology

It also stimulates the breakdown of liver and muscle glycogen (glucogenesis) to provide glucose for respiration.

questions.text: A steroid hormone which regulates glucose metabolism is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cortisone is a pregnene steroid hormone. It is a naturally-occurring corticosteroid metabolite that is also used as a pharmaceutical prodrug. Cortisol is converted by the action of the enzyme corticosteroid 11-beta-dehydrogenase isozyme 2 into the inactive metabolite cortisone, particularly in the kidneys.

Adrenal glands or (suprarenal glands) are two triangular endocrine glands, each of which covers the superior surface of a kidney. Each gland has two parts, the medulla and cortex. The medulla forms the grey core of the gland; it consists mainly of chromaffin tissue and is stimulated by the sympathetic nervous system to produce adrenaline and noradrenaline. The cortex is a yellowish tissue surrounding the medulla. It is stimulated by pituitary hormones (principally ACTH) to produce three kinds of corticosteroid hormones, which affect carbohydrate metabolism (e.g., cortisol), electrolyte metabolism (e.g., aldosterone), and the sex glands (oestrogens and androgens). Thus injury to adrenal cortex is not likely to affect the secretion of adrenaline.

Corticosterone, also known as 17-deoxycortisol and 11β,21-dihydroxyprogesterone, is a 21-carbon steroid hormone of the corticosteroid type produced in the cortex of the adrenal glands. In the very rare case of congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 17α-hydroxylase deficiency cortisol production is blocked.

11-Deoxycorticosterone, or simply deoxycorticosterone, also known as 21-hydroxyprogesterone, as well as desoxycortone, deoxycortone, and cortexone, is a steroid hormone produced by the adrenal gland that possesses mineralocorticoid activity and acts as a precursor to aldosterone. It is an active mineralocorticoid.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Endocrine glands regulate the neural activity but not vice versa
options.explanation: This is not correct.

options.text: Neurons regulate endocrine activity, but not vice versa
options.explanation: This is incorrect.

options.text: Endocrine glands regulate neural activity, and nervous system regulates endocrine glands.
options.explanation: The endocrine system links the brain to the organs that control body metabolism, growth and development, and reproduction. The endocrine system is regulated by feedback. For example, the hormones that are regulated by the pituitary gland, a signal is sent from the hypothalamus to the pituitary gland in the form of a “releasing hormone,” which stimulates the pituitary to secrete a “stimulating hormone” into the circulation. The stimulating hormone then signals the target gland to secrete its hormone. As the level of this hormone rises in the circulation, the hypothalamus and the pituitary gland shut down secretion of the releasing hormone and the stimulating hormone, which in turn slows the secretion by the target gland. This system results in stable blood concentrations of the hormones that are regulated by the pituitary gland.

options.text: Neither hormones control neural activity nor the neurons control endocrine activity
options.explanation: This is incorrect.

The stimulating hormone then signals the target gland to secrete its hormone. This system results in stable blood concentrations of the hormones that are regulated by the pituitary gland.

Progesterone is a steroid hormone released by corpus luteum to maintain pregnancy.

Prostaglandins are hormone-like substances that affect several bodily functions, including inflammation, pain and uterine contractions. Healthcare providers use synthetic forms of prostaglandins to treat several conditions. They also use medications to block the effects of prostaglandins.

Estrogen or oestrogen is a category of sex hormone responsible for the development and regulation of the female reproductive system and secondary sex characteristics. There are three major endogenous estrogens that have estrogenic hormonal activity: estrone, estradiol, and estriol.

questions.explanation: Epinephrine is synthesized from tyrosine which is a non-essential amino acid possessing a cyclic structure with a straight side chain bearing carboxylic and amino group. questions.text: Acromegaly is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acromegaly is a condition caused by an excess of growth hormone, which causes the overgrowth of bones in the face, hands and feet. A tumour on the pituitary gland is the most common cause of acromegaly.
