questions.text: Adrenaline directly affects the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adrenaline directly affects the SA node.The SA node, also known as the sinoatrial node, is the natural pacemaker of the heart. It is responsible for generating the electrical impulses that cause the heart to beat. Adrenaline increases the heart rate by binding to beta-adrenergic receptors on the SA node. This binding activates the adenylate cyclase enzyme, which leads to an increase in cAMP levels. cAMP is a second messenger that activates a number of proteins, including protein kinase A. Protein kinase A phosphorylates a number of proteins in the SA node, which ultimately leads to an increase in the heart rate.
B cells of Langerhans are pancreatic islet cells that produce insulin. Adrenaline can indirectly affect B cells of Langerhans by increasing the release of insulin from the pancreas.

options.text: Dorsal root of the spinal nerve
options.explanation: The dorsal root of the spinal cord contains sensory neurons that transmit signals from the periphery to the central nervous system. Adrenaline can indirectly affect the dorsal root of the spinal cord by increasing the firing rate of sensory neurons.

Epithelial cells of the stomach are responsible for secreting acid and other digestive enzymes. Adrenaline can indirectly affect epithelial cells of the stomach by decreasing the secretion of acid and other digestive enzymes.

questions.text: Mainly which type of hormones control the menstrual cycle in human beings?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The menstrual cycle is controlled by several endocrinal parameters. In the beginning of the cycle, FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) of the pituitary initiates the development of an ovarian follicle. A growing ovarian follicle gradually secretes an increasing amount of estrogen. This in turn leads to a sudden surge of LH secretion by the pituitary. As the LH (luteinising hormone) level in blood suddenly increases there is ovulation. Thus only FSH or LH cannot control all the events of the menstrual cycle. Progesterone is released by the corpus luteum after ovulation which actually prepares the uterus for a possible pregnancy. If there is no fertilisation, progesterone level falls and there is the beginning of a new cycle.

questions.text: Which set is similar?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: After ovulation many of the follicular cells remain in the collapsed Graafian follicle on the surface of the ovary. The antrum (cavity) of the collapsed follicle fills with a partially clotted fluid. The follicular cells enlarge and fill with a yellow pigment, lutein. Such a follicle is called a corpus luteum.

Sebum is an oily substance that is produced by the sebaceous glands in the skin. It is associated with the health and appearance of the skin, as it helps to moisturize and protect the skin from environmental damage. However, excess sebum production can lead to oily skin and clogged pores, which can contribute to the development of acne.

The bundle of His and the pacemaker are both parts of the electrical conduction system of the heart. The bundle of His is a bundle of muscle fibers that transmits electrical impulses from the atrioventricular (AV) node to the ventricles. The pacemaker is a group of specialized cells in the sinoatrial (SA) node that generate the electrical impulses that start the heartbeat.

Vitamin B7, also known as biotin, is a water-soluble vitamin that is essential for energy metabolism, cell growth, and fatty acid synthesis. Niacin, also known as vitamin B3, is a water-soluble vitamin that is essential for energy metabolism, cell signaling, and DNA repair.

questions.text: Melatonin is secreted by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Melatonin is a hormone secreted by the pineal gland and retinas of vertebrates. Melatonin secretion by the pineal gland is linked to the darklight cycle of the organism’s environment, being greatest at night and lowest by day. The hormone is involved in regulating certain diurnal and seasonal changes in the body, such as the reproductive cycle in seasonally breeding animals. It is used as a drug to treat sleep disorders and symptoms of jet lag.

The skin secretes several substances that are important for maintaining the health and integrity of the skin. These include sebum, which helps to moisturize and protect the skin from environmental damage; sweat, which helps to regulate body temperature and remove waste products from the body; and antimicrobial peptides, which help to protect the skin from infection. Additionally, the skin can produce vitamin D when exposed to sunlight, which is important for bone health and other bodily functions.

The pituitary gland secretes several hormones that play important roles in regulating various bodily functions. The anterior pituitary gland secretes hormones such as growth hormone, prolactin, follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, adrenocorticotropic hormone, and thyroid-stimulating hormone, which regulate growth, lactation, reproductive function, and the production of hormones by other endocrine glands. The posterior pituitary gland secretes oxytocin and antidiuretic hormone, which regulate water balance in the body, as well as social and sexual behavior.

The thyroid gland secretes several hormones that are important for regulating metabolism and other bodily functions. The two main hormones secreted by the thyroid gland are thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3). These hormones play important roles in regulating the body's metabolic rate, as well as growth and development, body temperature, and heart rate. The thyroid gland also secretes calcitonin, which helps to regulate calcium levels in the body.

The hormone is involved in regulating certain diurnal and seasonal changes in the body, such as the reproductive cycle in seasonally breeding animals. It is used as a drug to treat sleep disorders and symptoms of jet lag.

questions.text: Which gland secretes odorous secretion in mammals?

The prostate gland secretes a fluid that is an important component of semen. The fluid helps to nourish and protect sperm as they travel through the female reproductive tract. The prostate gland also produces an enzyme called prostate-specific antigen (PSA), which helps to liquefy semen after ejaculation. In some cases, elevated levels of PSA in the blood can be a sign of prostate cancer or other prostate-related conditions.

The anal glands are small paired sacs located on either side of the anus between the external and internal sphincter muscles. These sebaceous glands within the lining secrete a foul smelling liquid that is used for identification of members within a species. These glands are found in all carnivora except bears.

The liver secretes several substances that are important for digestion and other bodily functions. One of the main secretions of the liver is bile, which is produced in the liver and stored in the gallbladder until it is needed to help digest fats in the small intestine. The liver also produces a number of proteins, including albumin, which helps to regulate fluid balance in the body, and clotting factors, which are important for blood clotting. Additionally, the liver plays a key role in the metabolism of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins, and is responsible for detoxifying many harmful substances that enter the body.

The posterior lobe of the pituitary secretes oxytocin and vasopressin. Both of these are synthesized in hypothalamus are stored in posterior lobe of the pituitary, from here they are released upon stimulation

In man, it has no such role. The anterior lobe of the pituitary secretes FSH, LH, TSH, ACTH and STH. The posterior lobe of the pituitary secretes oxytocin and vasopressin.

questions.text: Secretion of progesterone by corpus luteum is initiated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Testosterone is a hormone that is primarily produced in the testicles in males and in smaller amounts in the ovaries in females. It plays an important role in the development of male reproductive tissues, such as the testicles and prostate gland, as well as secondary sexual characteristics such as increased muscle mass, body hair growth, and deepening of the voice. In females, testosterone also plays a role in maintaining bone density and muscle mass.

Thyroxine is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland. It plays an important role in regulating the body's metabolic rate, which affects how quickly the body uses energy and how quickly the body's cells function. Thyroxine levels can be affected by a variety of factors, including diet, stress, and certain medical conditions. If the thyroid gland produces too much or too little thyroxine, it can lead to a range of health problems, including weight gain or loss, fatigue, and other symptoms.

Melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) is a hormone that is produced by the pituitary gland in the brain. It plays an important role in regulating skin pigmentation by stimulating the production of melanin, which gives skin its color. MSH also plays a role in regulating appetite and other physiological processes.

Luteinsing Hormone (LH) in the male, induces the interstitial cells of the testes to produce male sex hormones named androgens such as testosterone. In the female, the luteinising hormone causes ovulation, secretion of female sex hormone, estrogen from the maturing ovarian follicle, and progesterone by the corpus luteum.

questions.text: The gonadotrophic hormones are secreted by the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gonadotrophic hormones are secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. (ii) Interstitial cell-stimulating hormone (ICSH): It activates the Leydig’s (interstitial) cells of the testis to secrete androgens. In female, it stimulates the corpus luteum of the ovary to secrete progesterone. In females, it is termed luteinizing hormone (LH).

questions.text: The hormone that stimulates the stomach to secrete gastric juice is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Enterokinase is an enzyme that is produced in the small intestine. It helps to activate other enzymes that are involved in the digestion of proteins. Specifically, enterokinase activates an enzyme called trypsinogen, which is produced by the pancreas. Once trypsinogen is activated, it becomes trypsin, which can then break down proteins into smaller peptides and amino acids that can be absorbed into the bloodstream.

Enterogastrone is a hormone that is produced in the small intestine. It helps to regulate the digestive system by slowing down the movement of food through the stomach and small intestine. This allows more time for nutrients to be absorbed into the bloodstream.

Gastrin is a peptide hormone produced by G cells in the pyloric antrum of the stomach, duodenum, and pancreas. It is released into the bloodstream when food enters the stomach, and stimulates the secretion of gastric acid, hydrochloric acid, and pepsin from the stomach lining. Gastrin also plays a role in gastric motility, and in the growth and differentiation of stomach cells.

It is produced and released by special cells in the kidneys in response to signals from the nervous system and other hormones. Renin helps to regulate blood pressure by triggering a series of chemical reactions that ultimately lead to the production of a hormone called angiotensin II, which causes blood vessels to constrict and blood pressure to increase.

Gastrin is released in response to the presence of food in the stomach, and it stimulates the production of stomach acid and enzymes that help to break down food. It also helps to relax the muscles in the stomach and small intestine, which allows food to move through the digestive system more easily.

Secretin is a hormone that is produced in the small intestine. It helps to regulate the digestive system by stimulating the pancreas to release bicarbonate, which is a base that helps to neutralize the acidic contents of the stomach as they enter the small intestine. Secretin also helps to reduce the production of stomach acid, which can help to prevent damage to the lining of the small intestine.

questions.text: The hormone which regulates the basal metabolism in our body is secreted from the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The adrenal cortex is the outer part of the adrenal gland. It produces a variety of hormones that regulate important bodily functions such as mineralocortecoids and glucocorticoids.

The pancreas is a large glandular organ located in the abdomen behind the stomach. It has two main functions:Exocrine function: The exocrine function of the pancreas produces pancreatic juice, a digestive fluid that contains enzymes that break down carbohydrates, fats, and proteins.Endocrine function: The endocrine function of the pancreas produces hormones that regulate blood sugar levels. These hormones include insulin and glucagon.

The pituitary gland, also known as the hypophysis, is a pea-sized gland located at the base of the brain. It is considered the "master gland" of the endocrine system because it secretes hormones that regulate the activities of other endocrine glands. The pituitary gland is divided into two main lobes: the anterior pituitary and the posterior pituitary.

The basal metabolism is the minimum amount of energy the body uses in order to maintain vital processes of the body. Generally, this expenditure of energy is expressed in terms of heat production per unit of body surface per day on the basal metabolic rate (BMR). Thyroid is the largest endocrine gland secreting three hormones thyroxine, triiodothyronine and calcitonin. Thyroxine and triiodothyronine control BMR of the body by regulating the rate of oxidation and production of energy.

questions.text: Hormone thyroxine, adrenaline and the pigment melanin are formed from
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hormones thyroxine, adrenaline and the pigment melanin are formed from tyrosine. Tyrosine is transformed into dopa through the enzyme tyrosinase. Then through different metabolic pathways it produces thyroxine, adrenaline, melanin etc.Tyrosine is a non-essential amino acid that is the precursor to thyroxine, adrenaline, and melanin. Tyrosine is synthesized in the liver from phenylalanine, another amino acid.

Proline is a non-essential amino acid that is important for the production of collagen, a protein that is essential for skin, bones, and cartilage. Proline is also involved in the production of creatine, an energy source for muscles.

Tryptophan is an essential amino acid that is the precursor to serotonin, a neurotransmitter that plays a role in mood, sleep, and appetite. Tryptophan is also the precursor to melatonin, a hormone that regulates sleep-wake cycles.

Glycine is a non-essential amino acid that is important for the production of proteins, collagen, and creatine. Glycine is also involved in the production of glutathione, an antioxidant that protects the body from damage.

Proline: Proline is important for the production of collagen and creatine. Glycine: Glycine is important for the production of proteins, collagen, creatine, and glutathione.

questions.text: Which hormone stimulates the secretion of milk in female?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: LH, or luteinizing hormone, is a hormone that is produced in the pituitary gland. In women, LH plays an important role in the menstrual cycle by stimulating the release of an egg from the ovary (ovulation). In men, LH stimulates the production of testosterone in the testes. LH levels can be measured in blood or urine tests to help diagnose certain medical conditions, such as infertility or pituitary gland disorders.

Prolactin (lactogenic hormone) is the hormone that stimulates milk secretion in lactating women. Prolactin stimulates mammary glands to produce milk. During pregnancy, prolactin helps in breast enlargement but due to the presence of high levels of progesterone and estrogen, milk secretion does not happen. Once women deliver, both progesterone and estrogen hormones levels drop and prolactin helps in secretion of milk.So the correct answer is "Prolactin".

Oxytocin is a hormone that is produced in the hypothalamus and released by the pituitary gland. It plays an important role in social bonding and reproductive functions. In women, oxytocin is involved in the contraction of the uterus during childbirth and the release of milk during breastfeeding. In both men and women, oxytocin is involved in the regulation of social behaviors, such as trust, empathy, and bonding.

Progesterone is a hormone that is produced in the ovaries in women and in smaller amounts in the testes in men. It plays an important role in the menstrual cycle and pregnancy. During the menstrual cycle, progesterone helps to prepare the uterus for pregnancy by thickening the lining. If pregnancy occurs, progesterone helps to maintain the pregnancy by preventing the uterus from contracting and shedding the lining. Progesterone also plays a role in the development of breast tissue and the regulation of bone density.

questions.explanation: Prolactin (lactogenic hormone) is the hormone that stimulates milk secretion in lactating women. Prolactin stimulates mammary glands to produce milk. During pregnancy, prolactin helps in breast enlargement but due to the presence of high levels of progesterone and estrogen, milk secretion does not happen. Once women deliver, both progesterone and estrogen hormones levels drop and prolactin helps in secretion of milk.So the correct answer is "Prolactin".

questions.text: Which one of the following endocrine glands stores its secretion in the extracellular space before discharging it into the blood?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The testes are a pair of male reproductive glands located in the scrotum. They produce testosterone, which is the primary male sex hormone. Testosterone is important for the development of male sexual characteristics, such as the growth of facial and body hair, deepening of the voice, and the development of muscles and bone mass. Testosterone also plays a role in sperm production and sex drive.

The thyroid gland secretes three hormones: thyroxine or tetraiodothyronine (T 4 ), triiodothyronine (T 3 ) and calcitonin. Thyroxine and Triiodothyronine are iodinated forms of the amino acid tyrosine. They are stored in the colloid that fills the follicles, and are released to the blood when needed. The storage occurs in an unusual place, the extracellular colloid.

The pancreas is a glandular organ located behind the stomach. It plays an important role in the digestive system and in regulating blood sugar levels. The pancreas produces enzymes that help to break down carbohydrates, proteins, and fats in the small intestine. It also produces hormones, such as insulin and glucagon, that help to regulate blood sugar levels. Insulin helps to lower blood sugar levels by promoting the uptake of glucose from the blood into cells, while glucagon helps to raise blood sugar levels by promoting the release of glucose from the liver.

The adrenal glands are two small glands that sit on top of the kidneys. They produce hormones that are essential for life, such as cortisol, adrenaline, and aldosterone. Cortisol helps to regulate the body's response to stress, and it also plays a role in regulating blood sugar levels, blood pressure, and immune system function. Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone that is released in response to stress or danger, and it helps to prepare the body for a "fight or flight" response. Aldosterone helps to regulate the balance of salt and water in the body, which is important for maintaining blood pressure and fluid balance.

Thyroxine and Triiodothyronine are iodinated forms of the amino acid tyrosine. They are stored in the colloid that fills the follicles, and are released to the blood when needed. The storage occurs in an unusual place, the extracellular colloid.

options.text: Continue to respond to the hormone without any difference

Therefore, they act at the cell surface. They bind to specific receptor molecules located on the surface of the cell membrane. Therefore, if receptor molecules are removed from target organs, then the target organ will not respond to the hormone.

questions.text: The immediate cause of induction of ovulation in human female is the large plasma surge of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: LH causes the ovulation and secretion of progesterone and oestrogen from ovarian follicles and corpus luteum. It is secreted by the anterior pituitary lobe at the end of the proliferative stage (day 14). In response to sudden release of LH from anterior pituitary lobe, egg from the mature follicle is released into the oviduct. The event is known as ovulation.

During the first day of cycle FSH begins to rise due to which few ovarian follicles are stimulated. One of the ovarian follicles matures and becomes graafian follicles. FSH stimulates the graafian follicle to secrete oestrogen.

During the secretory phase, the endometrium prepares for implantation of an embryo. Rising level of progesterone from corpus luteum acts on the oestrogen primed endometrium, causing the arteries to elaborate and converting the functional layer to a glandular secretory layer. The uterine glands enlarge, coil and begin secreting nutritious glycogen into the uterine cavity. If fertilisation has not occurred the corpus luteum begins to degenerate towards the end of the secretory phase as the LH level in blood declines. The progesterone level also falls.

Estradiol (E2) is an oestrogen steroid hormone and the major female sex hormone. It is involved in the regulation of the estrous and menstrual female reproductive cycles. Estradiol is responsible for the development of female secondary sexual characteristics such as the breasts, widening of the hips and a female-associated pattern of fat distribution. It is also important in the development and maintenance of female reproductive tissues such as the mammary glands, uterus and vagina during puberty, adulthood and pregnancy. It also has important effects in many other tissues including bone, fat, skin, liver, and the brain.

questions.explanation: LH causes the ovulation and secretion of progesterone and oestrogen from ovarian follicles and corpus luteum. It is secreted by the anterior pituitary lobe at the end of the proliferative stage (day 14). In response to sudden release of LH from anterior pituitary lobe, egg from the mature follicle is released into the oviduct. The event is known as ovulation.

questions.text: Testosterone is produced by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sertoli cells are elongated cells found in seminiferous tubules of testes. It nourishes the growing sperm cells during spermatogenesis.These cells function as phagocytes, consuming the cytoplasm left behind during spermatogenesis. Inhibin, aromatase binding protein, and anti-mullerian hormone are all secreted by these cells.
Leydig's cells are present between seminiferous tubules. They release testosterone when stimulated by LH. Testosterone causes the growth and development of germinal epithelium to form sperms.

Parietal cells (also known as oxyntic cells) are epithelial cells in the stomach that secrete hydrochloric acid (HCl) and intrinsic factor. These cells are located in the gastric glands found in the lining of the fundus and body regions of the stomach.

A pituitary gland is a small, pea-sized endocrine gland located at the base of brain below your hypothalamus. It releases several important hormones and controls the function of many other endocrine system glands.

questions.explanation: Interstitial cells (or Leydig‘s cells) are the cells interspersed between the seminiferous tubules of the testis. They secrete androgens including testosterone in response to stimulation by the luteinizing hormone from the anterior pitutary gland. Androgens produce and maintain male characteristics and stimulate germinal epithelium to undergo spermatogenesis.

questions.text: Gastric secretion is stopped by which hormone?

options.explanation: An enterogastrone is any hormone secreted by the mucosa of the duodenum in the lower gastrointestinal tract in response to dietary lipids that inhibits the caudal motion of the contents of chyme. Enterogastrone slow down stomach emptying by reducing the amount of acid produced. High acid content causes the valve between the stomach and intestine to relax, allowing food passage.

The primary stimulus for secretion of gastrin is the presence of certain foodstuffs, especially peptides, certain amino acids and calcium, in the gastric lumen.G-cells are neuroendocrine cells responsible for the synthesis and secretion of gastrin. They are primarily found in the pyloric antrum but can also be found in the duodenum and the pancreas.

Cholecystokinin, formerly called pancreozymin, is synthesised and secreted by enteroendocrine cells in the duodenum, the first segment of the small intestine. Its presence causes the release of digestive enzymes and bile from the pancreas and gallbladder, respectively, and also acts as a hunger suppressant.

Cholecystokinin is a peptide hormone of the gastrointestinal system responsible for stimulating the digestion of fat and protein. Cholecystokinin, formerly called pancreozymin, is synthesised and secreted by enteroendocrine cells in the duodenum, the first segment of the small intestine. Its presence causes the release of digestive enzymes and bile from the pancreas and gallbladder, respectively, and also acts as a hunger suppressant.

Gastrin stimulates the secretion of gastric juice. Cholecystokinin stimulates the release of enzymes in pancreatic juice and the release of bile from the gall bladder. Cholecystokinin is also known as pancreozymin.

options.explanation: Although enzymes are also protein in nature like hormones, enzyme protein is used for catalysing a reaction while hormones are regulatory proteins which regulate the amount of substances in blood.

Glycogenolysis it is the breakdown of glycogen by hormone glucagon in a result of low blood glucose level.

questions.text: Insulin is a
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vitamins are organic compounds that are essential for the normal functioning of the human body. They are micronutrients required in small amounts and play crucial roles in various physiological processes, such as growth, development, metabolism, and maintenance of overall health.

Lipids are a diverse group of organic compounds that are insoluble in water but soluble in organic solvents such as alcohol, ether, and chloroform. They are essential components of living cells and serve various functions in the body.

Insulin is secreted by endocrine tissue of pancreas the islets of langerhans . They secrete insulin in response to high blood glucose levels. Insulin is a protein that cannot pass through the cell membrane to stimulate mechanisms within the cell directly. It binds to the receptors in the cell membrane and effects indirectly through the action of Intracellular messengers. The receptors for insulin are present on cells in the liver, muscle tissue and adipose tissue. Insulin stimulates these cells to increase the rate of uptake of glucose.

Enzymes are biological molecules, specifically proteins or RNA molecules (ribozymes), that act as catalysts in various chemical reactions within living organisms. They speed up the rate of chemical reactions by lowering the activation energy required for the reaction to occur.

questions.explanation: Insulin is a peptide hormone, secreted by the b cells of the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas, that promotes the uptake of glucose by body cells, particularly in the liver (hepatocytes) and muscles (adipocytes), and thereby controls its concentration in the blood.

questions.text: Addition of a trace of thyroxine or iodine in water containing tadpoles will
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The larval stage of tadpoles refers to the early developmental stage of amphibians, specifically frogs and toads, after they hatch from eggs. During this stage, tadpoles have a distinctive appearance and physiological characteristics that are adapted for an aquatic lifestyle.

The function of thyroxine hormone in frogs is to control the metamorphosis of the frog. Metamorphosis is a biological process where some animals and insects undergo extreme physical changes.Metamorphosis is another word for the changes an animal makes during its life cycle. During a frog's metamorphosis, an egg will hatch into a tadpole, which will then develop back legs first, then front legs, and become a full grown adult frog.

The addition of a trace of thyroxine or iodine in water containing tadpoles can actually accelerate their metamorphosis rather than slowing it down. Iodine is an essential element required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones, including thyroxine. Adequate iodine levels are necessary for the normal functioning of the thyroid gland and the production of thyroid hormones. If tadpoles are exposed to iodine-deficient water, it can lead to reduced thyroid hormone production and delayed or impaired metamorphosis. Adding iodine to the water can help ensure sufficient levels for normal development.Therefore, the addition of a trace of thyroxine or iodine to water containing tadpoles is likely to promote and accelerate their metamorphosis rather than slowing it down.
The addition of a trace of thyroxine or iodine to water containing tadpoles should not pose a risk of immediate lethality if the concentration is appropriate.

questions.explanation: In 1912, Gudernatsch discovered that metamorphosis in a frog’s tadpole is increased by the thyroxine hormone which has iodine as the main constituent. If thyroxine or iodine is added to water having tadpoles in it, then it increases the rate of metamorphosis in the tadpole.

questions.text: Which hormone possesses an anti ­insulin effect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cortisol is secreted by the middle region of adrenal cortex in response to low blood glucose level by converting protein and fats into carbohydrates which are in turn converted to glucose.

Calcitonin is a hormone secreted by the thyroid gland. This hormone plays a minor but direct role in controlling extracellular levels of Ca+²ions . When the calcium level in the blood increases Calcitonin promotes Ca+² deposition into bones. When the level returns to normal thyroid gland decreases their secretion of Calcitonin. Calcitonin inhibits Ca+² absorption by the intestine and decreases its reabsorption by the kidney tubules allowing its excretion in urine. It also inhibits Potassium ion reabsorption by the kidney's tubules.

Oxytocin is a hormone is released by the posterior Pituitary lobe during childbirth and in nursing women. Stretching of the uterus and cervix during parturition is a strong stimulus for the release of Oxytocin. Low level of progesterone in blood to the end of pregnancy and neural stimuli of mother during childbirth also stimulates the release of Oxytocin.
Aldosterone is a hormone secreted by the adrenal glands that controls the reabsorption of sodium in the renal tubule of the nephron.

questions.explanation: Insulin decreases the level of glucose in the blood while cortisol (secreted by the middle region of the adrenal cortex) increases the blood glucose level by converting proteins and fats into carbohydrates which are, in turn, converted to glucose.

questions.text: MSH of the pars intermedia of the middle pituitary is responsible for
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The darkening of skin in lower vertebrates
options.explanation: Pars intermedia is the boundary between the anterior and posterior lobes of Pituitary gland. It contains three types of cells i.e basophils, chromophobe and colloid filled cysts. This area produces melanocytes stimulating hormones. It stimulates the synthesis of black pigment melanin in the skin and also causes dispersion of melanin granules in the pigment cells there by darkening the colour in certain animals e.g fishes, amphibians.

options.text: The light colouration of skin in lower vertebrates
options.explanation: MSH typically causes darkening of the skin in lower vertebrates, rather than lightening. In lower vertebrates, MSH released from the pars intermedia of the pituitary gland is responsible for darkening the skin coloration. MSH stimulates the dispersion of pigment granules (melanin) within specialized skin cells called melanophores. This dispersion of melanin leads to an overall darkening of the skin, often providing protective coloration or aiding in camouflage.
Only option A is correct as it only causes darkening of the skin, not lightening.

options.text: The darkening of skin in human beings
options.explanation: In human beings, the darkening of skin is primarily influenced by a pigment called melanin. The amount and type of melanin present in the skin determine its coloration.

questions.explanation: Pars intermedia is the boundary between the anterior and posterior lobes of the pituitary. It contains three types of cells, i.e. basophils, chromophobes and colloid filled cysts. This area produces melanocyte stimulating hormones or MSH. It stimulates the synthesis of the black pigment melanin in the skin and also causes dispersion of melanin granules in the pigment cells, thereby darkening the colour in certain animals (fishes; amphibians). In man, it has no such role.

questions.text: Information received by different receptors of body is analyzed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Autonomic nervous system consists of those fibres that connect CNS to the visceral organs such as heart , stomach , intestine . It is concerned with unconscious activities.
The central nervous system mainly consists of Brain and spinal cord. These two organs control all the voluntary and involuntary activities of the body.

It is part of the Autonomic nervous system and it mainly controls Autonomic functions during the state of rest. The Parasympathetic system returns the body function to normal after they have been altered by Sympathetic stimulation. Few cranial nerves including the vagus nerve together with nerves that arise from the sacral portion of the spinal cord form the Parasympathetic system.
The Sympathetic system controls various Autonomic functions in the state of emergency. It prepares the body for fight and flight. It consists of spinal nerves.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below.

questions.text: Ectoderm mainly gives rise to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The musculoskeletal system, including bones, muscles, and connective tissues, primarily develops from the mesoderm. Mesodermal cells differentiate into osteoblasts (bone-forming cells), myoblasts (muscle precursor cells), and various connective tissue cells that contribute to the development of the musculoskeletal system.

The three primitive germ layers serve as primitive tissue from which all body organs will be derived. Ectoderm form the structures of the nervous system and skin epidermis. Endoderm form the epithelial linings of the digestive, Respiratory and urogenital system and associated glands. Mesoderm forms virtually everything else i.e the cardiac and skeletal muscle, skeleton, and connective tissue.
The respiratory system forms from the endoderm, one of the germ layers. The endoderm gives rise to the lining of the respiratory tract, including the trachea, bronchi, and alveoli in the lungs.

The cardiovascular system, comprising the heart, blood vessels, and blood cells, also originates from the mesoderm. The mesodermal cells differentiate into cardiac myocytes (heart muscle cells), endothelial cells lining the blood vessels, and hematopoietic cells that form blood cells.

questions.explanation: Ectoderm mainly gives rise to nervous system and integumentary systems while mesoderm give rise to all other tissues of the body, including heart, the muscle system, the urogenital system, bones. and bone marrow and therefore the blood.

questions.text: A motor nerve carries impulses from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Motor neurons are part of the spinal nerves. Spinal nerves carry the motor neurons to the effectors.
Effectors do not carry messages to the CNS. Rather, it is the other way around. Cranial nerves are part of the CNS.
Effectors do not carry messages to the CNS. Rather, it is the other way around.

There are three types of neurons: Motor neurons:carry the impulse from CNS to the Effectors. Intermediate neurons:connect the Sensory and motor neuron present in CNS Sensory neurons: carry the impulse from receptors to the CNS. And two types of muscles: Effectors: These are the organs that receive command from CNS to take action in response to a stimulus. Receptors: These are either special cells or Dendrites of Sensory neurons that detect the stimulus.

questions.explanation: Efferent neurons are meant to conduct messages from CNS to the effectors as they are emerging from CNS. That is why they are known as motor neurons.

questions.text: Pressure is felt by your body immediately after it is appIied. This is achieved with the help of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are the receptors present in the skin dermis for the detection of Pressure and touch. They have free nerve endings.
These are present in the skin dermis for detection of change in temperature. They include ruffini ending for heat and krause end bulbs for cold.
These are the receptors in the skin that detect the pain. These are located in the epidermis of the skin. They are present throughout the body except in brain.

These are present in the taste buds. The tongue is covered with small bumps called papillae. Each papillae has many taste buds. The taste buds contain 50 to 100 Chemoreceptors that detect the chemical in the liquid we drink. The Chemoreceptors are covered with receptors proteins that detect these different chemicals.

questions.explanation: Mechanoreceptors detect stimuli of touch, pressure, hearing and equilibrium.They have free nerve endings.

questions.text: Which of the following produce response?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are the organs that receive command from CNS to take action in response to a stimulus.
The human nervous system is composed of nerves. Nerves contain nerve cells. These nerve cells are called neurons .

Peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) and spinal cord stimulation (SCS) are two types of electrical nerve stimulation. In either, a small pulse generator sends electrical pulses to the nerves (in peripheral nerve stimulation) or to the spinal cord (in spinal cord stimulation).
Brain is an organ of the CNS that controls the activity of the body.

questions.explanation: Effector is a structure or organ that brings about an action of 'effect' as a result of a stimulus received through a receptor which can come from the CNS or from a hormone.

questions.text: Stimulus of touch, pain, cold and heat are recognized differently because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: For each sensation, different form of nerve impulse is generated
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Nerve impulses, or action potentials, are electrical signals that are generated and transmitted by neurons to convey information. While the intensity and frequency of nerve impulses can vary based on the strength and duration of a stimulus, the basic form of the nerve impulse itself remains the same regardless of the type of sensation.

options.text: Different receptors are present for detection of stimuli
options.explanation: The dermis of the skin contains receptors for touch, pressure, temperature and pain. Meissner's corpuscles and Merkel disks for touch. Paccinian corpuscles for pressure. Mechanoreceptor for touch and pressure both and Thermoreceptors for change in temperature of body.

options.text: All nerves send their impulse to same part of brain
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Nerves carrying sensory information from different sensory receptors do not all send their impulses to the same part of the brain. Instead, sensory nerves project to specific regions of the brain that are dedicated to processing and interpreting different types of sensory information. For example, touch sensations are processed in somatosensory areas of the brain, while temperature sensations are processed in specific thermoreceptive regions.

options.text: Each stimulus acts on different parts of body
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While different stimuli may elicit sensations in different parts of the body, it is not the explanation for why the sensations of touch, pain, cold, and heat are recognized differently. The differentiation in sensory perception arises from the different types of sensory receptors and the neural pathways through which the signals are transmitted to the brain.

questions.explanation: Stimuli are always detected by specific receptors present in different parts of the body. Stimulus of touch, pain, cold and heat are recognized differently because these stimuli are detected by different receptors present in different parts of the body.

questions.text: Pick out the pressure receptors.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are present in the taste buds. The tongue is covered with small bumps called papillae. Each papillae has many taste buds. The taste buds contain 50 to 100 Chemoreceptors that detect the chemical in the liquid we drink. The Chemoreceptors are covered with receptor proteins that detect these different chemicals.
These are receptors present in eyes for the detection of light. For example rods and cone cells in the retina.

Mechanoreceptor detect changes in pressure, position, etc. They include: receptors for touch (Meissner's corpuscles in skin}, stretch or pressure (Pacinian's corpuscle in skin & baroreceptor in the wall of blood vessels).
These are present in the skin dermis for detection of change in temperature. They include ruffini ending for heat and krause end bulbs for cold.

questions.explanation: Mechanoreceptors are in the skin and on other organs that detect sensations of touch. They are called mechanoreceptors because they are designed to detect mechanical sensations or differences in pressure.

questions.text: Taste buds on the tongue are example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are present in the skin dermis for detection of change in temperature. They include ruffini ending for heat and krause end bulbs for cold.
These are receptors present in eyes for the detection of light. For example rods and cone cells in the retina.

Mechanoreceptors are specialized sensory receptors that respond to mechanical stimuli such as touch, pressure, vibration, and stretch. They are responsible for detecting and transmitting information related to mechanical forces and changes in the environment.

These are present in the taste buds. The tongue is covered with small bumps called papillae. Each papillae has many taste buds. The taste buds contain 50 to 100 Chemoreceptors that detect the chemical in the liquid we drink. The Chemoreceptors are covered with receptor proteins that detect these different chemicals.

questions.explanation: The taste receptors are located around the small structures known as papillae found on the upper surface of the tongue, these are chemoreceptors.

questions.text: Which of following is associated with sense of touch?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Paccinian corpuscles are encapsulated nerve endings present in the fatty layer deep into the skin. They are concerned with the sensation of pressure.
These are the receptors present in skin dermis for detection of Pressure and touch.They have free nerve endings.
Merkel disks are touch receptors in skin dermis. These consist of small,oval masses of flattened connective tissues.
Both mechanoreceptors and Merkel disks are associated with sense of touch.

questions.explanation: Merkel disks are touch receptors. Receptors for touch and pressure are also called mechanoreceptors.

questions.text: Non-neuronal cells encapsulating mainly axons of peripheral neurons are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microglia cells are the immune cells of the central nervous system and consequently play important roles in brain infections and inflammation. Microglia regulate brain development primarily through two routes: the release of diffusible factors and phagocytosis.

Oligodendrocytes are the myelinating cells of the central nervous system (CNS). Oligodendrocytes are specialised glial cells that wrap themselves around neurons present in the CNS. Oligodendrocytes are primarily responsible for maintenance and generation of the myelin sheath that surrounds axons.

Astrocytes also known as astroglia, are characteristic star-shaped glial cells in the brain and spinal cord. Astrocytes play a critical role in normal function of the mammalian nervous system. Astrocytes regulate synaptic transmission and plasticity, protect neurons against toxic compounds, and support metabolically to ensure their optimal functioning.
Specialised glial cells that form the myelin sheath that coat many axons. Cells surrounding the axons of some neurons, thus forming the myelin sheath.

questions.explanation: Some cells are attached with the neurons, along the length of axon that are specialized for the production of myclim sheath; these are type of ncuroglia cells in PNS named as Schwann cells.

questions.text: Myelinated,single and long fiber that takes message towards the cell body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A dendron is a branched protoplasmic extension of a nerve cell that propagates the electrochemical stimulation received from other neural cells to the cell body, or soma, of the neuron from which the dendrites project.The one which carry impulse towards cell body is called dendron, if it is a single fibre but if smaller fibres are present they are called dendrites
Axons are long thick nerve fibres surrounded by Schwann cells that have constant diameter and vary in length. The axon carries impulse from the cell body to the effectors.
Dendrites are the short, branching extensions of the neuron that receive signals from other neurons. They carry impulse towards the cell body.
Soma, also called the cell body, is a part of the neuron. It contains a mass of granular cytoplasm and cell membrane,single large nucleus,nucleolus,golgi apparatus,mitochondria and ribosome.

questions.explanation: A dendron is a branched protoplasmic extension of a nerve cell that propagates the electrochemical stimulation received from other neural cells to the cell body, or soma, of the neuron from which the dendrites project.The one which carry impulse towards cell body is called dendron, if it is a single fibre but if smaller fibres are present they are called dendrites

questions.text: The largest number of the cell bodies of neurons are found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The largest number of cell bodies of neurons in our body is found in the brain and spinal cord comes on the second number. The ganglia (which lie just outside the spinal cord) of the peripheral nervous system also have largest number of cell bodies.

The spinal cord is an organ of CNS. Spinal cord is a central cable of the nervous system. It is about 18 inches long and about half an inch in width. It is an elongated, hollow and cylindrical structure, lying in the neural canal of the vertebral column. It is continuous in front with the brain and tapers posteriorly, lying in the canal of urostyle. It is made up of a very large number of neurons. In the cross section, the spinal cord shows an inner grey matter and the outer white matter. The grey matter surrounds a central canal, containing cerebrospinal fluid.
The organs in the body that received stimulus from the internal and external environment are called sensory organs.
These are the organs that receive command from CNS to take action in response to a stimulus.

questions.explanation: Brain contains the highest number of neurons and spinal cord comes on second number.The ganglia (which lie just outside the spinal cord) of the peripheral nervous system also have largest number of cell bodies.

questions.text: Which of the following is not correct about myelin sheath?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The myelin sheath is composed of specialized lipids called phospholipids and glycolipids. These lipids are arranged in a bilayer, which forms a barrier that prevents the leakage of ions and helps to insulate the axon.

The myelin sheath is responsible for saltatory conduction, which is a process in which nerve impulses jump from node to node of Ranvier. Saltatory conduction allows nerve impulses to travel much faster than they would without the myelin sheath.

The myelin sheath does not conduct impulses. Impulses are conducted along the axon, which is the inner part of the nerve fiber. The myelin sheath insulates the axon and helps to speed up the conduction of impulses.
The myelin sheath is absent at the nodes of Ranvier. The nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath that allow ions to rush across the gap and generate a new nerve impulse.

questions.explanation: Composed of specialized lipids The myelin sheath is composed of specialized lipids called phospholipids and glycolipids. These lipids are arranged in a bilayer, which forms a barrier that prevents the leakage of ions and helps to insulate the axon. Responsible for saltatory conduction The myelin sheath is responsible for saltatory conduction, which is a process in which nerve impulses jump from node to node of Ranvier. Saltatory conduction allows nerve impulses to travel much faster than they would without the myelin sheath. Conduct impulses The myelin sheath does not conduct impulses. Impulses are conducted along the axon, which is the inner part of the nerve fiber. The myelin sheath insulates the axon and helps to speed up the conduction of impulses. Absence on node of Ranvier The myelin sheath is absent at the nodes of Ranvier. The nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath that allow ions to rush across the gap and generate a new nerve impulse. Therefore, the answer is conduct impulses.

questions.text: Myelin sheath:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The myelin sheath is found in all axons, but the thickness of the myelin sheath varies depending on the type of nerve fiber. Myelinated nerve fibers have a thick myelin sheath, while unmyelinated nerve fibers do not have a myelin sheath. Myelinated nerve fibers are able to conduct nerve impulses much faster than unmyelinated nerve fibers.

The myelin sheath is found in all axons, but it is not found in all dendrites. Dendrites are the short, branching extensions of a neuron that receive signals from other neurons. Axons are the long, slender projections of a neuron that transmit signals to other neurons.

The myelin sheath acts as an insulator for the axon, which helps to prevent the leakage of ions and increases the speed of nerve impulse conduction. Nerve impulses are electrical signals that travel along the axon of a neuron. When a nerve impulse reaches a node of Ranvier, a gap in the myelin sheath, the ions rush across the gap and generate a new nerve impulse. This process of saltatory conduction allows nerve impulses to travel much faster than they would without the myelin sheath.

The myelin sheath is a proteinaceous substance, which means that it is made up of proteins. Proteins are long chains of amino acids that are essential for all aspects of cell structure and function. The proteins in the myelin sheath help to insulate the axon and speed up the conduction of nerve impulses.

questions.explanation: Is proteinaceous The myelin sheath is a proteinaceous substance, which means that it is made up of proteins. Proteins are long chains of amino acids that are essential for all aspects of cell structure and function. The proteins in the myelin sheath help to insulate the axon and speed up the conduction of nerve impulses. Speeds up conduction The myelin sheath acts as an insulator for the axon, which helps to prevent the leakage of ions and increases the speed of nerve impulse conduction. Nerve impulses are electrical signals that travel along the axon of a neuron. When a nerve impulse reaches a node of Ranvier, a gap in the myelin sheath, the ions rush across the gap and generate a new nerve impulse. This process of saltatory conduction allows nerve impulses to travel much faster than they would without the myelin sheath. Found in all dendron The myelin sheath is found in all axons, but it is not found in all dendrites. Dendrites are the short, branching extensions of a neuron that receive signals from other neurons. Axons are the long, slender projections of a neuron that transmit signals to other neurons. Found in all axons The myelin sheath is found in all axons, but the thickness of the myelin sheath varies depending on the type of nerve fiber. Myelinated nerve fibers have a thick myelin sheath, while unmyelinated nerve fibers do not have a myelin sheath. Myelinated nerve fibers are able to conduct nerve impulses much faster than unmyelinated nerve fibers.

questions.text: Which one of the following are also known as efferent neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are neurons that carry signals from the sensory receptors to the CNS. Sensory neurons are responsible for transmitting information about the external and internal environment to the brain. They are also known as afferent neurons.

Associative neurons are neurons that connect sensory and motor neurons within the CNS. Associative neurons are responsible for processing information and integrating it into motor responses. They are also known as inter or relay neuron.

Motor neurons are also known as efferent neurons.Efferent neurons are neurons that carry signals away from the central nervous system (CNS) to the muscles and glands. Motor neurons are responsible for initiating and controlling voluntary movement.
Mixed neurons are neurons that have both sensory and motor axons. Mixed neurons are found in the autonomic nervous system, which controls involuntary functions such as heart rate and digestion.

questions.explanation: Motor neurons are also known as efferent neurons.Efferent neurons are neurons that carry signals away from the central nervous system (CNS) to the muscles and glands. Motor neurons are responsible for initiating and controlling voluntary movement.

questions.text: Regeneration is possible in all parts of neurons except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The other parts of the neuron, the dendrites, dendrons and axons, can regenerate, but only under certain conditions.A dendron is a branched protoplasmic extension of a nerve cell that propagates the electrochemical stimulation received from other neural cells to the cell body neuron from which the dendrites project.

The other parts of the neuron, the dendrites, dendrons and axons, can regenerate, but only under certain conditions. Axons are the long, slender projections of the neuron that transmit signals to other neurons.

The other parts of the neuron, the dendrites , dendrons and axons, can regenerate, but only under certain conditions. Dendrites are the short, branching extensions of the neuron that receive signals from other neurons.

The part of a neuron in which regeneration is not possible is the cell body.The cell body, also known as the soma, is the central part of the neuron and contains the nucleus and other essential organelles. When a neuron is damaged, the cell body can die, and if it does, the neuron cannot regenerate.

questions.explanation: Extensions of neuron have the ability to regenerate only when if its cell body is intact becauses the nucleus of the neurons is present in it but cell body is devoid of that property.The cell body, also known as the soma, is the central part of the neuron and contains the nucleus and other essential organelles. When a neuron is damaged, the cell body can die, and if it does, the neuron cannot regenerate.

questions.text: It is not true about cell body of neuron:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell body of a neuron is main nutritionaI part. It is the part where biosynthesis of required materials is taking place. It also provides the genetic information for the regeneration of neuronal processes.

Axoplasm is the cytoplasm of an axon, and the cell body does not contain axons. The cell body contains the nucleus, mitochondria, Golgi apparatus, and other organelles necessary for the cell's metabolism.

Cell body of a neuron is main nutritionaI part. It is the part where biosynthesis of required materials is taking place. It also provides the genetic information for the regeneration of neuronal processes.
If the cell body of the neuron remains intact, it can regenerate axonal and dendrite ibres; but neurons once mature, do not divide any further. Therefore this statement is correct.

questions.explanation: The following is not true about the cell body of a neuron:It contains axoplasm.Axoplasm is the cytoplasm of an axon, and the cell body does not contain axons. The cell body contains the nucleus, mitochondria, Golgi apparatus, and other organelles necessary for the cell's metabolism. The other three options are all true about the cell body of a neuron: It is the main nutritional part of the neuron. It contains the necessary organelles for biosynthesis of material. It is responsible for the regeneration of dendrite and axon fibers. The cell body is the central hub of the neuron, and it is essential for the neuron's function. It receives signals from dendrites, processes them, and then sends signals out through the axon. The cell body also contains the necessary organelles for the neuron to produce energy and repair itself. Therefore only option (b) is incorrect about cell body of neuron.

questions.text: Which organelle cannot be found in synaptic knob of a neuron?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria are present in the synaptic knob, but they are not essential for neurotransmitter release. Mitochondria provide energy for the cell, but neurotransmitter release can still occur in the absence of oxygen.

Ribosomes are also present in the synaptic knob, but they are not essential for neurotransmitter release. Ribosomes produce proteins, but neurotransmitter release does not require the synthesis of new proteins.
Secretary vesicles are essential for neurotransmitter release. They store and release neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft.

The organelle that cannot be found in the synaptic knob of a neuron is the nucleus.The synaptic knob is the bulbous end of a presynaptic axon, where neurotransmitters are stored and released. It contains synaptic vesicles, mitochondria, and other organelles necessary for neurotransmitter release, but not the nucleus.The nucleus is the control center of the cell, and it contains the DNA that codes for the cell's proteins. Since neurons do not divide once they are mature, they do not need a nucleus in the synaptic knob.

questions.explanation: The organelle that cannot be found in the synaptic knob of a neuron is the nucleus.The synaptic knob is the bulbous end of a presynaptic axon, where neurotransmitters are stored and released. It contains synaptic vesicles, mitochondria, and other organelles necessary for neurotransmitter release, but not the nucleus.The nucleus is the control center of the cell, and it contains the DNA that codes for the cell's proteins. Since neurons do not divide once they are mature, they do not need a nucleus in the synaptic knob. The other organelles listed, mitochondria, ribosomes, and secretory vesicles, are all present in the synaptic knob. Mitochondria provide energy for neurotransmitter release, ribosomes produce proteins for the synaptic vesicles, and secretory vesicles store and release neurotransmitters. Therefore, the correct answer is nucleus

questions.text: Which of the following is most abundant type of neurons in CNS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are responsible for transmitting information from the senses to the CNS. They are the least abundant type of neuron in the CNS, making up only about 10% of the total population.

Interneurons also known as associative neurons are the most abundant type of neuron in the central nervous system (CNS). They are responsible for processing and relaying information between sensory neurons and motor neurons. Interneurons also play a role in higher-order cognitive functions such as memory, learning, and decision-making.
Motor neurons are responsible for transmitting information from the CNS to the muscles. They are also less abundant than interneurons, making up only about 1% of the total population.
Neuroglial cells are not neurons, but rather non-neuronal cells that support and protect neurons. They are the most abundant type of cell in the CNS, outnumbering neurons by about 10 to 1.

questions.explanation: Interneurons are the most abundant type of neuron in the central nervous system (CNS). They are responsible for processing and relaying information between sensory neurons and motor neurons. Interneurons also play a role in higher-order cognitive functions such as memory, learning, and decision-making. Therefore the correct answer is associative neuron (inter neuron).

questions.text: Which one of the following gives a tree-like appearance to neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sensory neurons are psedounipolar and we define dedrons for them. Sensory neurons lack true dendrites, except for the terminal portion it is structurally and functionally of the axon type. Hence, it is called a dendron.

The soma, also known as the cell body or perikaryon, is the central part of a neuron. It contains the nucleus and most of the cell's organelles. The soma is responsible for maintaining the neuron's structure and carrying out essential cellular functions. However, it does not give a tree-like appearance to neurons.

Dendrites are the branch-like extensions that emanate from the cell body of a neuron. They are specialized to receive incoming signals and information from other neurons. Dendrites have a tree-like appearance because they extend in various directions, resembling the branches of a tree. These branches provide a large surface area for the neuron to receive input from multiple sources, which is crucial for integrating and processing information within the nervous system.

The axon is another long, slender projection of a neuron, but unlike dendrites, it extends away from the cell body and is responsible for transmitting electrical signals (action potentials) to other neurons, muscles, or glands. The axon can be quite long and can also have branches, but it does not give a tree-like appearance to neurons. Instead, it often looks more like a single elongated fiber.

questions.explanation: Explanation of each option: a) Dendron: The sensory neurons are psedounipolar and we define dedrons for them. Sensory neurons lack true dendrites, except for the terminal portion it is structurally and functionally of the axon type. Hence, it is called a dendron. b) Soma: The soma, also known as the cell body or perikaryon, is the central part of a neuron. It contains the nucleus and most of the cell's organelles. The soma is responsible for maintaining the neuron's structure and carrying out essential cellular functions. However, it does not give a tree-like appearance to neurons. c) Dendrites: Dendrites are the branch-like extensions that emanate from the cell body of a neuron. They are specialized to receive incoming signals and information from other neurons. Dendrites have a tree-like appearance because they extend in various directions, resembling the branches of a tree. These branches provide a large surface area for the neuron to receive input from multiple sources, which is crucial for integrating and processing information within the nervous system. d) Axon: The axon is another long, slender projection of a neuron, but unlike dendrites, it extends away from the cell body and is responsible for transmitting electrical signals (action potentials) to other neurons, muscles, or glands. The axon can be quite long and can also have branches, but it does not give a tree-like appearance to neurons. Instead, it often looks more like a single elongated fiber. In conclusion, the correct option is c) Dendrites because they are the extensions that give a tree-like appearance to neurons, branching out from the cell body and receiving incoming signals from other neurons.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a part of neuron?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The axon is a long, thin fiber that transmits electrical signals away from the cell body. It is an important part of neuron.
Dendrites are also part of the neuron. They are short, branched fibers that receive electrical signals from other neurons.
Nissl granules are ribosomes that are responsible for protein synthesis. They are present in granular form inside the cell body.

The answer is Synapse.A synapse is the junction between two neurons where electrical signals are transmitted from one neuron to another. It is not a part of the neuron itself, but rather a specialized structure that allows neurons to communicate with each other.

questions.explanation: The answer is Synapse.A synapse is the junction between two neurons where electrical signals are transmitted from one neuron to another. It is not a part of the neuron itself, but rather a specialized structure that allows neurons to communicate with each other.The other three options, axon, cell body, and Nissl granules, are all parts of the neuron. The axon is a long, thin fiber that transmits electrical signals away from the cell body. The cell body contains the nucleus and other organelles that are essential for the cell functions. Nissl granules are ribosomes that are responsible for protein synthesis. Therefore synapse is the correct answer.

questions.text: _ is considered as chief structural and functional unit of nervous system.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell is too broad of a term. While neurons are cells, they are not the only type of cell in the nervous system. Other types of cells, such as glial cells, also play important roles in the nervous system.

The chief structural and functional unit of the nervous system is the neuron. Neurons are specialized cells that transmit information throughout the body through electrical and chemical signals. They are responsible for all of our senses, thoughts, and movements.
Nephron is the functional unit of the kidney, not the nervous system.

Brain is the largest organ in the nervous system and is responsible for many important functions, but it is not the only part of the nervous system. Neurons are also found in the spinal cord and throughout the body in the form of nerves.

questions.explanation: The chief structural and functional unit of the nervous system is the neuron. Neurons are specialized cells that transmit information throughout the body through electrical and chemical signals. They are responsible for all of our senses, thoughts, and movements. The other options are not as accurate because: Cell is too broad of a term. While neurons are cells, they are not the only type of cell in the nervous system. Other types of cells, such as glial cells, also play important roles in the nervous system. Nephron is the functional unit of the kidney, not the nervous system. Brain is the largest organ in the nervous system and is responsible for many important functions, but it is not the only part of the nervous system. Neurons are also found in the spinal cord and throughout the body in the form of nerves.

questions.text: Which one of the following is common to all neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A cell body which contains a nucleus
options.explanation: The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron. Hence is present in all neurons.

The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron. Hence is present in all neurons. Some neurons are non-myelinated, meaning they will lack Nodes of Ranvier.

The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron, hence is present in all neurons. Not all neurons have a thick myelin sheath. Neurons have a range of thickness in myelin sheaths, defined based on how fast must a particular neuron conduct an impulse.

questions.explanation: The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron hence is present in all neurons.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a component of reflex arc?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Afferent nerve (sensory neuron) has pain-sensitive endings in the skin and a long fiber leading to the spinal cord. That neuron stimulates.

The sensory neuron also makes a synapse on association neurons not involved in the reflex that carry signals to the brain, informing it of the danger. The reflex arc is the pathway of passage of impulse during a reflex action. Reflex action is a type of involuntary action. The direction of stimulus is from receptors to sensory neurons to associative (association/relay) neurons and then through motor neurons to the effectors.

Efferent nerves (motor neurons) form the somatic nervous system, which controls voluntary movements, which are under the conscious control of the body, involving skeletal muscles. Motor neurons also form the autonomic nervous system, which controls involuntary responses by influencing organs, glands, and smooth muscles.

These are the structures that respond when they are stimulated by impulses coming via motor neurons. The principal effectors are glands, which respond by secreting; and muscles which respond by contracting. The flow of information through the nervous system is explained with the help of a reflex arc.

The axon of the motor neuron carries action potentials to (4) muscles,causing them to contract and withdraw the body part from the damaging stimulus. The sensoryneuron also makes a synapse on association neurons not involved in the reflex that carry signals tothe brain, informing it of the danger.

questions.text: Depolarization is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The influx of potassium ions (K+) into a cell does not lead to depolarization. It can have the opposite effect, contributing to repolarization or hyperpolarization.

Depolarization is primarily the result of an influx of sodium ions (Na+). This influx of positively charged sodium ions into a cell, particularly a neuron, reduces the cell's membrane potential, making it less negative. This change in membrane potential is a key step in the initiation of an action potential.
Efflux of potassium ions (K+) can contribute to repolarization, as potassium ions leaving the cell make the membrane potential more negative, bringing it closer to its resting state.
Efflux of sodium ions (Na+) would not contribute to depolarization. Sodium ions leaving the cell would tend to restore the resting membrane potential.

questions.explanation: The influx of Na+ increases the concentration of positively charged ions in the cell and causes depolarization, where the potential of the cell is higher than the cell's testing potential. The sodium channels close at the peak of the action potential, while potassium continues to leave the cell.

questions.text: Which of the following causes the hyperpolarization across neurolemma?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The distribution of charge alone is a general concept related to the separation of positive and negative charges across the cell membrane but does not specifically cause hyperpolarization.
The influx of sodium ions (Na+) tends to depolarize the neurolemma, making it less negative. It does not cause hyperpolarization.

Hyperpolarization across the neurolemma is primarily caused by the late closing of potassium (K+) channels. When these channels remain open for an extended period, excessive potassium efflux continues, making the cell membrane more negatively charged than its resting potential. This increased negativity results in hyperpolarization, making it more difficult for the neuron to generate another action potential.
The outward diffusion of sodium ions (Na+) would contribute to depolarization, not hyperpolarization. Hyperpolarization is characterized by an increased negative charge.

questions.explanation: Hyperpolarization is a change in a cell's membrane potential that makes it more negative. It is the opposite to that of a depolarization. It is mainly caused by late closing of K+ channels.

questions.text: Which one of the following sets of ions is necessary for the transmission of nerve impulses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The transmission of nerve impulses primarily involves the movement of sodium ions (Na+) into the cell and potassium ions (K+) out of the cell. This movement of ions is essential for generating and propagating action potentials in neurons.
While calcium ions (Ca2+) play a role in neurotransmitter release at the synapse, they are not primarily responsible for the transmission of nerve impulses along the length of a neuron.
Calcium ions (Ca2+) are involved in various cellular processes, including neurotransmitter release, but they are not the primary ions responsible for transmitting nerve impulses.
Magnesium ions (Mg2+) are not directly involved in the transmission of nerve impulses. Sodium (Na+) and potassium (K+) ions are the primary ions responsible for this process.

questions.explanation: Sodium and potassium ions plays key role in the conduction of nerve impulse by their recpective movement across the membrane.

questions.text: Which of the following will restore original ion gradients and RMP after hyperpolarization in neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Potassium channels play a role in repolarization, but they do not directly restore the original ion gradients or RMP after hyperpolarization.

Although a repolarised neuron fibre has the same as that polarized neuron fibre but has different ionic distribution, it has more K+ outside and Na+ inside. So the repolarised nerve fibre undergoes a refractory period of a few milliseconds. The original ionic distribution is restored by the sodium-potassium pump which actively transports Na+ ions out and K+ ions in.
Sodium channels are involved in depolarization and action potentials, not in restoring the ion gradients and RMP after hyperpolarization.
Calcium channels are not typically involved in restoring the resting membrane potential after hyperpolarization; they have different functions related to calcium signaling in neurons.

questions.explanation: In neurolemma, a specialized ATP dependant Na+/K+ pump is present which will restore original ion gradient and hence the resting membrane potential.

questions.text: Conduction of action potentials from one node of Ranvier to another in myelinated neurons is through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hyperpolarization refers to a change in membrane potential that makes the neuron less excitable, and it is not the primary mechanism for conduction in myelinated neurons.
Depolarization is the initial change in membrane potential that triggers an action potential but is not how action potentials propagate between nodes in myelinated neurons.
The resting membrane potential is the baseline electrical charge across a neuron's membrane and is not the mechanism by which action potentials are conducted between nodes in myelinated neurons.

In myelinated neurons, action potentials "jump" or "leap" from one node of Ranvier to another in a process called saltatory conduction. This allows for faster and more efficient transmission of nerve impulses.

questions.explanation: The correct option is: d) Saltatory Conduction Explanation of each option: a) Hyperpolarization: Hyperpolarization refers to a change in the membrane potential of a neuron or any excitable cell, making it more negative than its resting membrane potential. This occurs when there is an increase in the efflux of positively charged ions (e.g., potassium) or an influx of negatively charged ions (e.g., chloride). Hyperpolarization makes it more difficult for the neuron to generate an action potential, as the membrane potential becomes further away from the threshold required to trigger an action potential. b) Depolarization: Depolarization is the opposite of hyperpolarization. It refers to a change in the membrane potential of a neuron or any excitable cell, making it less negative (closer to zero) than its resting membrane potential. This occurs when there is an influx of positively charged ions (e.g., sodium) or a decrease in the efflux of positively charged ions (e.g., potassium). Depolarization brings the membrane potential closer to the threshold required to trigger an action potential. c) Resting Membrane Potential: The resting membrane potential is the electrical charge across the cell membrane of a neuron when it is not actively transmitting signals. In a typical neuron, the resting membrane potential is around -70 millivolts (mV), meaning the inside of the neuron is 70 mV more negative than the outside. This electrical potential exists due to the uneven distribution of ions (such as sodium, potassium, chloride) across the cell membrane, which is maintained by ion channels and ion pumps. d) Saltatory Conduction: Saltatory conduction is the process by which action potentials "jump" from one node of Ranvier to another in myelinated neurons. Myelinated neurons have a fatty substance called myelin sheath wrapped around their axons, which acts as an insulating layer. The myelin sheath covers the axon except for periodic gaps called the nodes of Ranvier. At these nodes, the axon membrane is exposed. When an action potential is initiated at the initial segment of the axon (near the cell body), it rapidly propagates along the myelinated axon, but it "skips" or jumps over the myelinated regions. The myelin sheath prevents the flow of ions across the membrane, which saves energy and speeds up the conduction of the action potential. This saltatory conduction allows the action potential to travel much faster than it would in an unmyelinated neuron, where the action potential has to be regenerated at every point along the axon. In conclusion, the correct option is d) Saltatory Conduction because it describes how action potentials "jump" from one node of Ranvier to another in myelinated neurons, significantly speeding up the transmission of nerve impulses along the axon.
